EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of and with a cold front moving across the upper Ohio Valley, with some severe storms across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. A brief tornado touched down near the border of Carroll and Columbiana counties, and a stronger downburst occurred in East Liverpool in Columbiana county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey found a EF-0 tornado touched down in Carroll county and crossed into extreme western Columbiana county west of Summitville. See Storm Data for Columbiana county for the remainder of this tornado. The tornado touched down near the intersection of Apollo and Merline Roads, where a metal roof of a grain silo was removed and a small barn damaged. The path continued near Marigold Road where a trampoline was wrapped around a power pole. Damage to trees was then found along Nature and Lewis Roads, before the storm passed into Columbiana county near Acker Road. The total path of the tornado was 3.2 miles, with the maximum path width 75 yards. Estimated maximum wind speed was 75 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of and with a cold front moving across the upper Ohio Valley, with some severe storms across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately unstable, but weakly sheared environment was in place across the region. Several outflow boundaries acted on these ingredients, allowing for pulse-type thunderstorms to develop. The majority remained below severe limits. However, a few reports of damage were received in northwestern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported a tree down on County Road Q between County Road 2 and the Wood County Line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the peak heating of the day with strong upper level winds in place. The main threat from these storms was damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary situated over interstate 80 became the focus for thunderstorm development as several upper level disturbances rounded a flattening ridge over the plains. These storms eventually congealed into several lines, one near the western Pennsylvania/Ohio border and another further back into Ohio southern lower Michigan, that moved southeastward through the evening of the 25th and early on the 26th. In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ohio department of highways reported trees down along Route 751.

These storms eventually congealed into several lines, one near the western Pennsylvania/Ohio border and another further back into Ohio southern lower Michigan, that moved southeastward through the evening of the 25th and early on the 26th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of convection moved across portions of central Indiana and Ohio during the late evening hours of June 12, lasting into the early morning hours of June 13. The main threats from these storms were damaging winds and isolated weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado briefly touched down on the west side of Highway 257 southwest of Radnor. The tornado initially hit two barns, destroying one and heavily damaging another. One barn consisted of cinder block walls and tin roofing. Much of the cinder block collapsed, while roofing of this structure with some of the attached cinder block and other bard roofing and walls was lifted across Highway 257 where much of the material was wrapped around trees along the Scioto River. Large portions of the barn roofing was lofted over the tree line along the Scioto River and deposited in the back yards of residences along River Road, on the east side of the river, a distance of two tenths of a mile away. Tree damage along the south side of the brief tornado track was lying in a northeast orientation which was inward toward the tornado track which confirms the convergent circulation. Other damage along the path included snapped large trees, complete destruction of a small shed, and large amounts of debris wrapped around trees. Corn stalks were found in yards of residence on the east side of the Scioto River, with the nearest corn fields nearly a quarter of a mile away. Based on the damage surveyed, the maximum wind speed of this tornado was approximately 85 miles per hour.

A thunderstorm complex that developed in conjunction with a strengthening surface low over northern Indiana propagated westward towards the region late on the 12th. Out ahead of this system, strong speed shear and instability contributed to supercellular development along a warm frontal boundary that bisected the region. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported before the bow echo reached eastern Ohio very early on the 13th. This bow echo, with a history of widespread wind damage, then crossed through the forecast area through 6am, causing several reports of downed trees and wires through much of eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and southwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported multiple large trees down in Jefferson Township. Trees were down in about an acre of an area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The left 2 lanes and shoulder of Interstate 270 west at Interstate 71 had approximately 6 to 8 inches of water across it due to heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving showers and thunderstorms developed around a low pressure area during the daytime heating. Some of these storms moved over the same areas repeatedly, causing heavy rainfall and flash flooding to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was crossing several roads in the Russia and Houston Areas due to heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed in the morning ahead of an approaching cold front. The main threat from these storms was damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was downed and numerous other trees were damaged at Losantiville Country Club due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving band of showers and thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall across portions of southwestern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was stranded in 3 feet of water under a railroad overpass along State Route 4 due to heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the peak heating of the day. One of these storms became severe. The main threat from these storms was damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered trees were reported down from Moorefield Township to the Champaign County line due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed in conjunction with a strengthening surface low over northern Indiana propagated westward towards the region late on the 12th. Out ahead of this system, strong speed shear and instability contributed to supercellular development along a warm frontal boundary that bisected the region. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported before the bow echo reached eastern Ohio very early on the 13th. This bow echo, with a history of widespread wind damage, then crossed through the forecast area through 6am, causing several reports of downed trees and wires through much of eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and southwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported small trees knocked down on Township Road 399.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low was located over the northern Great Lakes. While no distinct fronts or waves were noted, enough instability and shear was in place across the region to allow for isolated thunderstorm development. The majority of these storms produced brief, heavy rainfall and gusty winds. Isolated large hail was reported in the Ottawa area.

An outflow boundary situated over interstate 80 became the focus for thunderstorm development as several upper level disturbances rounded a flattening ridge over the plains. These storms eventually congealed into several lines, one near the western Pennsylvania/Ohio border and another further back into Ohio southern lower Michigan, that moved southeastward through the evening of the 25th and early on the 26th. In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.

A cold front crossed the Ohio Valley during the day. Rainfall over the area briefly changed to freezing rain and sleet before transitioning to heavy snowfall. Accumulations of 4 to 6 inches were common across southwest Ohio, tapering off slightly to around 4 inches in central Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on surrounding observations, 5 to 7 inches of snow was estimated to have fallen over the county. The highway department east of Eaton measured 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system moved northeast across the region the morning of November 26th into the morning of the 27th. A period of snow accumulated 1 to 4 inches across much of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland before mixing with and changing to rain. The precipitation remained all snow in a band from Forest county Pennsylvania to Columbiana county Ohio where a general 6 to 10 inches of snow fell. In Garrett county Maryland low level cold air was trapped allowing for freezing rain to accumulate from a quarter to one half inch across the eastern half of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches the morning of the 26th before becoming a wintry mix, then plain rain in the afternoon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large limb was reported down in Donnelsville due to thunderstorm winds.

Doppler Radar estimates of 3 to 4 inches of rain fell over southern Crawford and central Richland Counties between 830 PM and 1130 PM with rainfall rates as much as 4.8/hr around 930 PM. This excessive rainfall led to flash flooding in the hardest hit communities of Galion and Mansfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe storms and heavy rains which hit Galion from Wednesday night and early Thursday morning caused more than $200,000 in damages in Galion. $100,000 in damages occurred to the Gospel Baptist Church. City firefighters evacuated seven residents on Sixth Avenue when knee-high flood waters rose around 11 p.m. at the single-story apartments. A residence on Grant Street flooded, leaving the home a total loss, according to Galion Police Department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving showers and thunderstorms moved into Ashtabula County with the heaviest rain falling between 8 am and noon. A COOP observer in Dorset measured over 4 inches of rain during this time. The ground, already saturated from a near record rainfall for the month of June, could not absorb the rain and subsequently produced excessive runoff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Upwards of 4.25 inches of rain fell across Ashtabula County, overwhelming drainage systems, flooding streets and roads, submerging basements and damaging property in communities south of Interstate 90. A Conneaut motorist was unhurt when her vehicle drove into high water on Broad Street. No weather-related injuries were reported, according to safety personnel. In the Ashtabula Harbor, water was knee-high at the intersection of West 9th Street and Thayer Avenue flooding the neighborhoods of Treelane, Saybula and Westshore drives. Several roadways and state highways were closed because of high water. Route 193 was shut down between routes 307 and 167 in the Dorset area, and Route 531 just east of Geneva-of-the-Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across northern Ohio with weak low pressure over the region. One thunderstorm produced golf ball size hail in Mount Gilead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported golf ball size hail in Mount Gilead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along a weak cold front moving southeast across northern Ohio.  Some of the stronger thunderstorms produced hail up to quarter size in Hancock, Erie, Lorain, and Cuyahoga Counties.  The most noteworthy storm of the day covered the ground with quarter size hail in Elyria in Lorain County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported quarter size hail south of Mt. Cory.

A series of slow moving thunderstorms moved into Richland County on Saturday during the evening hours. Rainfall rates exceeded 2 inches per hour, most notably a storm over the city of Mansfield. Flash flooding resulted across the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Doppler Radar estimated over two inches of rain had fallen in southern and central Richland County between 430 pm and 600 pm. All Mansfield emergency agencies were called out as flash floods put parts of the city under water, delaying many of the weekend���s events, shutting down several area roads and even damaging a few. The city���s street department reported flooding closed down 12 intersections. All but the Park Avenue East underpass and Fourth Street and Bowman Avenue were opened by 10 p.m. Five vehicles stalled out trying to navigate rising waters at the underpass on Park Avenue East where within 20 minutes the water rose three feet. Fourth Street was blocked off at Bowman Avenue after flooding washed the blacktop down to the brick. The road has been under construction since Thursday. Mulberry Street also was closed at the railroad crossing after the heavy rain popped a piece of metal up off the tracks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary front over northern Ohio between the 10th and 13th brought one to three inches of rain to the region. The heaviest rain fell along and just south of the Ohio River Valley/Lake Erie watershed boundary with two to three inches of rain. As a result most of the central and eastern rivers remained in banks. The following rivers experienced flooding during this event: Scioto, Sandusky, Maumee, Portage, and the Killbuck Creek. The hardest hit basin was the Blanchard River (upstream of the Maumee River) over Hancock County. Rainfall amounts of 2.5 to 3 inches during a 36 hour period resulted in rapid river rise. At the river gage in Findlay, OH the river climbed from a low flow rate of 60 cfs on the 10th to a rate of 7,600 cfs on the 12th. The river exceeded major flood stage of 13.5 feet on the evening of the 11th and reached a stage of 14.1 feet by the morning of the 12th. At this stage several secondary streets and major thoroughfares were closed. Due in part to some recent mitigation work, no first floor inundation to structures was observed. A few basements and crawlspaces were inundated, but no detectable damage resulted from the flooding. Elsewhere across the region the flooding was restricted to closing local roads with minimal impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain pushed the Blanchard River in Findlay into major flood. The river crested just over 14 feet, more than a half a foot above major flood stage. The impacts were mainly isolated along the business district where the water inundated streets and some low lying basements. Homes along the Eagle Creek were threatened, but none were inundated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary situated over interstate 80 became the focus for thunderstorm development as several upper level disturbances rounded a flattening ridge over the plains. These storms eventually congealed into several lines, one near the western Pennsylvania/Ohio border and another further back into Ohio southern lower Michigan, that moved southeastward through the evening of the 25th and early on the 26th. In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.

A thunderstorm complex that developed in conjunction with a strengthening surface low over northern Indiana propagated westward towards the region late on the 12th. Out ahead of this system, strong speed shear and instability contributed to supercellular development along a warm frontal boundary that bisected the region. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported before the bow echo reached eastern Ohio very early on the 13th. This bow echo, with a history of widespread wind damage, then crossed through the forecast area through 6am, causing several reports of downed trees and wires through much of eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and southwestern Pennsylvania.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small line of training thunderstorms brought torrential rainfall to southern Trumbull County on the evening of August 8th. The airmass over the region at the time was warm and humid which had led to rainfall rates over 2 inches per hour in earlier storms. The thunderstorms were spotty, however in this even the storms trained over the same area for two hour. A measured rainfall of 4.5 inches between 4 and 5 PM in Newton Falls was in the range of a 1000 year rain event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The flash flooding on August 8th put most of the Newton Falls Community Park  under water along with several homes and businesses. State Route 534 along with several other roads were closed including Crossroads with Warren Ravenna Road, Taft Avenue and Wilson Avenue, had water standing as high as the mailbox of one residence. The storm sewer systems were overwhelmed causing backups into hundreds of homes and businesses' basements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary boundary remained the focus for afternoon showers and thunderstorms again on June 30th. While shear and instability remained meager, with cape values of 1000j/kg or less, some storms were able to support themselves to severe levels. A little more than a dozen wind damage and hail reports combined made up the afternoon. But, once again, training thunderstorms produced flooding problems, this time over Allegheny county in Pennsylvania.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to ten inches of water were reported inside the business of Cincinnati Pool and Patio with about 3 feet of water in the parking lot. This flooding height corresponds to the height of the river gauge of nearby Mill Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching disturbance in the morning. The rainfall persisted and was heavy at times due the very moist air mass in place. The result of these showers and thunderstorms was locally heavy rainfall, flooding, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wolford-Maskill Road at Martin Welch Road was covered by high moving water due to heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado developed over Guernsey county in Ohio with a line a showers associated with a strong cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak and brief tornado occurred in Guernsey county near Helena, which is about 4 miles southwest of Byesville. A deputy recorded the tornado as it moved through trees. The tornado was rated an EF0, with maximum winds of 65 MPH. The maximum path width was 50 yards, with a path length of 0.20 of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved east across Lake Erie dragging a cold front across northern Ohio.  Showers and thunderstorms developed in advance of the front with a few of thunderstorms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a few trees and large limbs near Leavittsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved east across Lake Erie dragging a cold front across northern Ohio.  Showers and thunderstorms developed in advance of the front with a few of thunderstorms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved east across Lake Erie dragging a cold front across northern Ohio.  Showers and thunderstorms developed in advance of the front with a few of thunderstorms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and limbs in Bazetta and Howland Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed in conjunction with a strengthening surface low over northern Indiana propagated westward towards the region late on the 12th. Out ahead of this system, strong speed shear and instability contributed to supercellular development along a warm frontal boundary that bisected the region. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported before the bow echo reached eastern Ohio very early on the 13th. This bow echo, with a history of widespread wind damage, then crossed through the forecast area through 6am, causing several reports of downed trees and wires through much of eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and southwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported tree limbs down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported widespread, significant wind damage across the county. Three utility trailers were pushed across the parking lot at the Emergency Operations Center.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured a 64 mph wind gust. Tree limbs in excess of eight inches were blown down by the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary situated over interstate 80 became the focus for thunderstorm development as several upper level disturbances rounded a flattening ridge over the plains. These storms eventually congealed into several lines, one near the western Pennsylvania/Ohio border and another further back into Ohio southern lower Michigan, that moved southeastward through the evening of the 25th and early on the 26th. In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.

A quasi-stationary boundary remained the focus for afternoon showers and thunderstorms again on June 30th. While shear and instability remained meager, with cape values of 1000j/kg or less, some storms were able to support themselves to severe levels. A little more than a dozen wind damage and hail reports combined made up the afternoon. But, once again, training thunderstorms produced flooding problems, this time over Allegheny county in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly moving across the Ohio Valley was the focus for the development of scattered thunderstorms, some of which were severe, through the evening hours of the 16th of April. While cloud cover kept instability weak, some deep layer shear helped maintain some storms to severe levels. Quarter sized hail and wind damage was reported in portions of western Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio, and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported a tree down on power lines in Newton Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front crossed the region during the early morning hours of November 12th producing a widespread snowfall. In the higher elevations of Preston, Tucker, and Garrett counties up to 4 inches of snow fell.

An outflow boundary situated over interstate 80 became the focus for thunderstorm development as several upper level disturbances rounded a flattening ridge over the plains. These storms eventually congealed into several lines, one near the western Pennsylvania/Ohio border and another further back into Ohio southern lower Michigan, that moved southeastward through the evening of the 25th and early on the 26th. In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Highways reported trees down along Route 658.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed in conjunction with a strengthening surface low over northern Indiana propagated westward towards the region late on the 12th. Out ahead of this system, strong speed shear and instability contributed to supercellular development along a warm frontal boundary that bisected the region. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported before the bow echo reached eastern Ohio very early on the 13th. This bow echo, with a history of widespread wind damage, then crossed through the forecast area through 6am, causing several reports of downed trees and wires through much of eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and southwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Highways reported flooding on State Route 83, just south of Coshocton.

The precipitation remained all snow in a band from Forest county Pennsylvania to Columbiana county Ohio where a general 6 to 10 inches of snow fell. In Garrett county Maryland low level cold air was trapped allowing for freezing rain to accumulate from a quarter to one half inch across the eastern half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a stalled frontal boundary draped across Lake Erie and the upper Ohio Valley.  Locally heavy rainfall was reported across northern Ohio with reports of up 6 inches in Lake County.  A tornado touched down in eastern Cuyahoga County and caused some damage at a small college.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched just northwest of the Ursuline College campus around 3:35 am.  The tornado continued south southeast across a part of the campus, crossed Fairmount Boulevard and finally lifted as it moved across a country club.  Substantial damage was done to a gymnasium on the campus.  The tornado caused a wall to collapse and tore off a part of the roof.  Other buildings nearby sustained lesser amounts of roof damage.  Many trees were also toppled or snapped along the damage path which was up to 200 yards in width and around a mile and a third in length.  There was also evidence of straight line wind damage in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing low pressure system along the Gulf of Mexico coast spread its precipitation north into the mid Ohio Valley during the overnight hours of the 25th into the 26th.   Southeast Ohio was located on the northwestern fringe of the precipitation shield. ||Most of southeast Ohio changed over to rain during the daylight hours on the 26th before ending again as a period of snow. However, even though the precipitation was not as heavy, mostly snow fell across Perry County, with a lull at times during the daylight hours on the 26th.  The snow diminished well before dawn on the 27th, to just some lingering flurries or snow showers.||Snow accumulations of 3 to 5 inches fell across Perry County in a period spanning 24 hours or slightly longer.  Roads became hazardous during the evening hours of the 26th.  A county level 1 emergency was declared.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter solstice arrived on the 21st with near record warmth in southeast Ohio.  Temperatures reached into the upper 60s to lower 70s.   In a fast wind flow above the ground, a squall line raced northeast into the area after midnight.  The gusty showers had a few embedded thunderstorms and preceded a larger area of rain.  Some minor wind damage occurred.||The 1 to 2 inches of rain from the 21st into the 22nd caused the larger streams to swell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some minor flooding occurred in eastern Washington County along Duck Creek and the Little Muskingum River on the 22nd.   The gauge at Bloomfield on the Little Muskingum River crested between 18 and 19 feet during the afternoon.  Bankfull is 16 feet.  State Route 26 was covered by water.   Duck Creek also exceeded bankfull at Macksburg and Whipple.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snow was enhanced over west central Ohio as a surface low pressure system tracked from the Mississippi Valley towards southeast Ohio. Warmer air pulled in with the low pressure significantly cut snowfall totals south of the Interstate 70 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Uniopolis received 5.8 inches of snow. Cridersville and Wapakoneta received 5.5 inches, while New Knoxville and St. Marys received 5.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heavy band of snow fell south and southwest of metropolitan Dayton, associated with an upper level disturbance and a weak surface trough of low pressure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter near Fairborn reported 3 inches of snow. The CoCoRaHS observer and county garage near Xenia both measured 2.5 inches of accumulating snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the Ohio Valley during the day. Rainfall over the area briefly changed to freezing rain and sleet before transitioning to heavy snowfall. Accumulations of 4 to 6 inches were common across southwest Ohio, tapering off slightly to around 4 inches in central Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer 3 miles east-northeast of Gordon recorded 5.7 inches of snow. Over the far northern part of the county significantly less snow fell, around 2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front crossed through the Ohio Valley during the evening of November 11th, changing rain showers to a round of snow showers for the early morning of November 12th. It produced 1-2 inches of snow over portions of western and central Ohio and was the first measurable snow of the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An inch of snow was measured in Union. At the Dayton International Airport, 0.9 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked east of the Appalachians during the afternoon of November 26th. At the same time, a mid-level trough crossed the great lakes and helped squeeze out rain and snow showers over portions of the Ohio Valley, which quickly changed to snow showers in the evening. Accumulations southeast of the I-71 corridor were generally 1 to 3 inches, with some higher amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three and a half inches of snow was measured in Chillicothe and three miles northwest of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across northern Ohio during the evening hours of November 17th.  A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed in advance of this front causing significant damage in some areas.  At least three tornadoes touched down in Wood County.  The strongest of the three, an EF2, crossed into Lucas County.  A fourth tornado occurred in Ottawa County.  All of the tornadoes downed trees and damaged homes and buildings.  Strong non thunderstorm winds also occurred behind the cold front through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sheriffs Office reported trees and power lines down from Fulton to Chesterville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter solstice arrived on the 21st with near record warmth in southeast Ohio.  Temperatures reached into the upper 60s to lower 70s.   In a fast wind flow above the ground, a squall line raced northeast into the area after midnight.  The gusty showers had a few embedded thunderstorms and preceded a larger area of rain.  Some minor wind damage occurred.||The 1 to 2 inches of rain from the 21st into the 22nd caused the larger streams to swell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down near the intersection of Route 1 and 52.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down northwest of North Baltimore along Quarry Road to the west of Liberty Hi Road. Damage elsewhere along the damage path consisted of a few downed trees.  The parent thunderstorm responsible for this tornado later produced another tornado near Jerry City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight wind field behind an arctic cold front combined with temperatures in the single digits to near zero produced wind chills from 10 to 20 degrees below zero. The lowest readings were during the early morning hours of January 22nd and 23rd across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills across eastern Ohio were around 10 degrees below zero on the mornings of January 22nd and 23rd. The coldest reported readings was 14 below zero in Salem, Ohio on the morning of the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across northern Ohio during the evening hours of November 17th.  A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed in advance of this front causing significant damage in some areas.  At least three tornadoes touched down in Wood County.  The strongest of the three, an EF2, crossed into Lucas County.  A fourth tornado occurred in Ottawa County.  All of the tornadoes downed trees and damaged homes and buildings.  Strong non thunderstorm winds also occurred behind the cold front through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A EF0 tornado crossed into Lucas County from Wood County just to the west of Interstate 280 in Northwood.  The tornado continued to the northeast and intensified to EF1 intensity as it moved across the Pearson Metropark.  It tore the roof off of a house and damaged another home in this area.  The tornado briefly intensified to EF2 as it neared Seaman Street in Oregon.  The tornado made a left turn at that time and finally lifted as it crossed Corduroy Road north of Oregon.  The tornado damaged many homes in Oregon.  Most of the damage was from lost roofing or siding but at least two homes were damaged enough to be considered destroyed.   The tornado was on the ground for over four miles in Lucas County.  The damage path was up to a 100 yards in width.  Dozens of trees were downed along the damage path and many vehicles were damaged by the strong winds or flying debris.

Strong non thunderstorm winds also occurred behind the cold front through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts snapped at least nine trees on Woodland Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system moved across the region, bringing strong winds aloft across the Ohio Valley. Showers that developed along and ahead of a cold front associated with this storm system were capable of producing damaging winds and isolated weak tornadoes. This line of showers producing the damage across southwestern Ohio continued past midnight on October 31st and into the early morning hours of November 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down and snapped numerous healthy pine trees that were 12 to 18 inches in diameter. A large cottonwood tree was also snapped. Several tree branches were blown down on roofs, with some siding damage to a garage. Based on the damage surveyed, the maximum wind speed of this tornado was estimated to be 95 miles per hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down east of Elmore along West Yeasting Road just to the west of State Route 590.  The tornado moved northeast and briefly intensified to EF1 intensity as it crossed State Route 590.  A barn near this location was leveled.  The tornado continued on the ground for another half mile or so and then lifted.  The damage path was around three quarters of a mile in length and no more than 75 yards in width.  Damage consisted mainly of a few downed trees and limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across northern Ohio during the evening hours of November 17th.  A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed in advance of this front causing significant damage in some areas.  At least three tornadoes touched down in Wood County.  The strongest of the three, an EF2, crossed into Lucas County.  A fourth tornado occurred in Ottawa County.  All of the tornadoes downed trees and damaged homes and buildings.  Strong non thunderstorm winds also occurred behind the cold front through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down near Lime City Road just to the north of U.S. Route 20 in Perrysburg.  The tornado continued east northeast reaching EF2 intensity as it neared the intersection of State Route 795 and Oregon Road.  Two warehouse buildings had walls blown down and lost large sections of roofing.  A gas station at the intersection of State Route 795 and Oregon Road was heavily damaged.  Several vehicles at the gas station were damaged.  Other businesses in the immediate vicinity also sustained roof damage.  After causing extensive damage to a couple homes in a residential neighborhood just north of State Route 795, the tornado weakened to EF0 intensity.  It then remained on the ground as either an EF0 or EF1 tornado for another four and half miles before moving into Lucas County near Northwood.  It damaged several more homes and some apartment buildings before entering Lucas County.  Most of this damage was from lost roofing and siding.  Dozens of trees were downed along the damage path which was just over seven miles long and up to a 100 yards in width in Wood County.  Many vehicles including a couple of recreational vehicles were damaged by the strong winds or flying debris.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down east of Jerry City near the intersection of Main Street and Huffman Road.  The tornado then continued east for around one and a third miles and lifted after crossing Cloverdale Road.  A mobile home along Main Street was destroyed and a couple of other homes and buildings in the area lost roofing and siding.  Damage along the remainder of the damage path consisted of mainly downed trees.  The parent thunderstorm responsible for this tornado earlier produced a tornado near North Baltimore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the region during the late evening hours of October 31st. A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed in advance of the front and caused some damage. Over two dozen reports of damaging winds were reported. The Tiffin area of Seneca County was especially hard hit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed toppled a large tree and some limbs.  A home was damaged by the fallen tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms with high rainfall rates produced flash flooding in the city of Tiffin. Coming off a near record wet June, the saturated ground could not hold the volume of runoff produced by an estimated 2 to 3 inches within an hour and a half. Flooding was confined to the urban portions of Tiffin with only nuisance flooding along some county roads outside of the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Tiffin, water reached between two and three feet behind Tiffin Mall on Westwood and Northwood drives. The residential area between Kroger and Miami Street was most affected as many homes had up to a foot of water in their basements. Roadways in Seneca County with high water included CR 26, east of SR 53, SR 18, west of 224 and SR 53 south of Fort Seneca.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a stalled frontal boundary draped across Lake Erie and the upper Ohio Valley.  Locally heavy rainfall was reported across northern Ohio with reports of up 6 inches in Lake County.  A tornado touched down in eastern Cuyahoga County and caused some damage at a small college.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of the 20th, specifically between 230 am and 530 am, torrential rain fell over extreme northeast Cuyahoga County and most of Lake County. The showers were enhanced by the warm and moist air over Lake Erie, and subsequently produced rainfall rates that exceeded the storm drainage systems in any populated communities affected.  Rainfall reports ranged from 3.44 in Madison to 6 inches in Fairport Harbor. In Kirtland, an observer measured 2.20 inches between 3 am and 5 am. The rain came after two previous weeks of wet weather which had raised the water table and saturated the ground. The rainfall produced flooding in over 500 basements with the majority being sanitary sewer backups. One basement's wall caved in causing structural damage to the house. Overall 13 properties in Mentor were classified as having suffered major structural damage. The most damage was reported in western Lake between Mentor and Wickliffe.  In Kirtland Hills the Baldwin Road sustained structural damage from washout. In Concord 50 homes were flooded and were not due to sewer backups.  In Cuyahoga County, Euclid was one of the highly affected areas, with more than 5 inches of rain falling in a short amount of time. That left some residents with anywhere from several inches to several feet of water in their homes. The wastewater treatment plant was under 20 feet of water and much of the equipment needed to be replaced. In all over 3,500 properties sustained some form of flood inundation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of deepening low pressure moved northeast across the Ohio Valley on December 14th.  The low eventually moved across the southeast corner of Ohio during the evening hours.  Snow associated with this low spread into northwestern Ohio around midnight on the 14th.  The snow intensified around daybreak when visibilities dropped below a mile.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow with visibilities less than a half mile and snowfall rates in excess of an inch per hour occurred during the daylight hours.  The snow eventually tapered to flurries during the evening.  Snowfall totals over Hancock, Wood, Sandusky, Ottawa and Lucas Counties ranged from 6 to 8 inches.  A peak total of 8.0 inches was reported at Point Place in Lucas County.  Officially, 7.4 inches of snow was measured at Toledo Express Airport in Lucas County.  Many accidents were reported during this event .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of deepening low pressure moved northeast across the Ohio Valley on December 14th.  The low eventually moved across the southeast corner of Ohio during the evening hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front well in advance of low pressure across the Great Lakes produced a mix of winter precipitation across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, western Pennsylvania, and Garrett county Maryland. Areas from Oil City to Dubois Pennsylvania received over one quarter inch of ice due to freezing rain, with amounts greater than one quarter inch also reported across Garrett county Maryland. Across the rest of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia up to one tenth of an inch of ice accumulated, with a light accumulation of sleet and snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light coating of freezing rain up to one tenth of an inch fell across much of eastern Ohio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving low pressure center brought a mix of winter precipitation to the upper Ohio Valley. Freezing rain accumulations of over one quarter inch occurred across portions of Tucker county West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland. A light coating of freezing rain mixed with sleet fell over eastern Ohio and the remainder of northern West Virginia. Across western Pennsylvania light mixed freezing rain, sleet and snow produced up to one tenth of an inch of ice with 1 to 2 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light coating of freezing rain mixed with sleet at times fell across a good portion of eastern Ohio, including the Zanesville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Albert Clipper type system moved across the Ohio Valley spreading light to moderate snow across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland during the 25th. Northwest winds behind the system produced snow showers into the day the 26th. Snowfall for the entire event along and south of a line from Lisbon, Ohio to Pittsburgh and Latrobe, Pennsylvania was 3 to 5 inches over the 2 days. During the 2 days across the higher elevations of northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland and the Laurel and Chestnut Ridges in Pennsylvania 4 to 7 inches fell. Areas from the northern suburbs of Pittsburgh to Interstate 80 in Pennsylvania received 1 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A general 3 to 4 inches of snow fell across much of eastern Ohio from midday on January 25th to the late afternoon of the 26th. The highest amounts fell across parts of Jefferson county, where up to 5 inches of snow fell over the 2 days.

On Sunday and Monday March 24th and 25th, snow and sleet along with some rain spread through the region, with significant snow found over metropolitan Dayton and to the northwest. Surface low pressure moved from the Tennessee Valley to Ohio during the overnight. This low got added influence with an upper low tracking east through Kentucky during the late night and early part of Monday. The system occluded over the state and large snowfall amounts were recorded north and west of Dayton. Drier air in the occlusion was pulled into central Ohio, limiting snowfall totals significantly in this area. Much of the early part of the event started as rain in the southern portion of the CWA and snow totals were notably lower in southern Ohio, southeast Indiana, and northern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four and a half inches of snow was measured on the east side of Beavercreek, and by a public report out of Fairborn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Sunday and Monday March 24th and 25th, snow and sleet along with some rain spread through the region, with significant snow found over metropolitan Dayton and to the northwest. Surface low pressure moved from the Tennessee Valley to Ohio during the overnight. This low got added influence with an upper low tracking east through Kentucky during the late night and early part of Monday. The system occluded over the state and large snowfall amounts were recorded north and west of Dayton. Drier air in the occlusion was pulled into central Ohio, limiting snowfall totals significantly in this area. Much of the early part of the event started as rain in the southern portion of the CWA and snow totals were notably lower in southern Ohio, southeast Indiana, and northern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report from Marysville indicated 5.5 inches of snow had fallen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifting through the region brought a period of snow, sleet, and freezing rain to northwest Ohio during the early morning hours of February 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow and sleet overspread the area during the early morning hours of February 22nd before ending as a period of freezing drizzle. Total snow accumulations across the county were generally near 1 inch, with ice accretions less than 0.10 inches. Temperatures in the 20s allowed the light snow and ice to accumulate on area roadways. This led to a few accidents and slide-offs across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the afternoon hours of February 19th.  The snow showers intensified during the evening hours of the 19th and early morning hours of the 20th.  Visibilities at times were less than a quarter mile with snowfall rates in excess of an inch per hour.  Westerly winds gusted to as much 30 mph during this event and caused much blowing and drifting.  A swath of 6 inches or more of snow stretched from eastern Lake County across Ashtabula County and into northwestern Pennsylvania.  Several accidents were reported and travel along Interstate 90 was hampered by this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the afternoon hours of February 19th. The snow showers intensified during the evening hours of the 19th and early morning hours of the 20th. Visibilities at times were less than a quarter mile with snowfall rates in excess of an inch per hour. Westerly winds gusted to as much 30 mph during this event and caused much blowing and drifting. Six or more inches of snow was reported over much of eastern Lake County. Several accidents were reported and travel along Interstate 90 was hampered by this event.

A strengthening low pressure system tracking east through the Ohio Valley region dropped between 5 and 9 inches of heavy snow on northwest Ohio, mainly during the late afternoon and evening hours of March 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread the area during the late afternoon hours of March 5th and became heavy at times during the evening and early overnight hours. Total snow accumulations across the county generally ranged between 5 and 8 inches. There was a report of 8.0 inches in Antwerp. The intensity of the heavy snow and sub-freezing surface temperatures allowed the snow to accumulate on roadways. This resulted in hazardous travel conditions across the region with numerous school closings the morning of March 6th.

A cold front swept east across the upper Ohio Valley on March 20th.  Cold west to northwest winds developed behind the front causing lake effect snow showers to develop.  The snow showers were intermittent and finally ended during the morning hours of the 22nd.  The snow was heavy at times, especially during the afternoon and evening hours of the 21st when visibilities were less than a half mile at times.  Winds gusted to more than 20 mph causing some blowing and drifting.  Snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were reported across portions of inland Ashtabula County.  A peak total of 12.3 inches was reported at Pierpont.  Some accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept east across the upper Ohio Valley on March 20th. Cold west to northwest winds developed behind the front causing lake effect snow showers to develop. The snow showers were intermittent and finally ended during the morning hours of the 22nd. The snow was heavy at times, especially during the afternoon and evening hours of the 21st when visibilities were less than a half mile at times. Winds gusted to more than 20 mph causing some blowing and drifting. Snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were reported across portions of inland Ashtabula County. A peak total of 12.3 inches was reported at Pierpont. Some accidents occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system lifting northeast through the Midwest and Great Lakes brought a period of moderate freezing rain to northwest Ohio on February 26th. Ice accretions near a tenth of an inch were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate rain overspread the region on February 26th. Surface temperatures hovered near freezing during the morning hours which allowed the rain to freeze on mainly elevated surfaces. Ice accretions near a tenth of an inch were common on trees, power lines, bridges, and overpasses. This combined with wind gusts to 30 mph aided in downing a few tree limbs. There were also reports of isolated power outages and accidents across the region.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate rain overspread the region on February 26th. Surface temperatures hovered near freezing during the morning and early afternoon hours which allowed the rain to freeze on mainly elevated surfaces. Ice accretions near a tenth of an inch were common on trees, power lines, bridges, and overpasses. This combined with wind gusts to 30 mph aided in downing a few tree limbs. There were also reports of isolated power outages and accidents across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm system over the Plains moved into the mid-Mississippi Valley, spreading a wintry mix of precipitation across the Ohio Valley during the evening hours of February 21 into the morning hours of February 22. With sub-freezing temperatures near the surface, the precipitation started off as a mix of sleet/snow early on and then transitioned to mainly freezing rain as warmer air aloft nosed into the region. Snow and sleet accumulations were generally less than an inch, and ice accumulations from freezing rain generally ranged from one to two tenths of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer just north of Eaton measured .3 inches of ice accumulation. A spotter northeast of the city also measured .3 inches of accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system tracking northeast through the Ohio Valley region dropped between 5 and 9 inches of heavy wet snow on northwest Ohio, mainly during the morning hours of March 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow overspread the area during the late evening hours of March 24th before tapering off late the next morning. Total snow accumulations across the county generally ranged between 7 and 9 inches. There was a report of 8.3 inches in Van Wert. The intensity of the heavy snow, gusty winds, and reduced visibilities resulted in hazardous travel conditions across the region. Numerous school closings were reported the morning of March 25th, along with a few slide-offs and accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain spread north into the region ahead of a warm front during the evening hours of January 27th. Ice accretions near a tenth of an inch were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing rain quickly changed over to all freezing rain during the evening hours of January 27th. Light ice accretions were reported, generally near a tenth of an inch. Secondary roads became slick and hazardous which resulted in a few accidents across northwest Ohio. The freezing rain changed over to rain and drizzle during the early morning hours of January 28th as temperatures warmed above freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across the Upper Ohio Valley early on January 31st. Cold westerly winds behind the cold front caused lake effect snow showers to develop before daybreak on the 31st. The snow showers intensified around midday with periods of moderate to heavy snow during the afternoon and evening hours. Visibilities were near zero at times with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour. The westerly winds gusted in excess of 30 mph causing much blowing and drifting. Intermittent snow continued into the 1st with the snow shower activity diminishing from west to east during the evening. Snowfall totals ranged from a few inches in western Cuyahoga County to over a foot in portions of Ashtabula County. Peak totals included: 14.3 inches at Pierpont in Ashtabula County; 9.6 inches at Thompson in Geauga County and 9.0 inches at Garfield Heights in Cuyahoga County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Sunday and Monday March 24th and 25th, snow and sleet along with some rain spread through the region, with significant snow found over metropolitan Dayton and to the northwest. Surface low pressure moved from the Tennessee Valley to Ohio during the overnight. This low got added influence with an upper low tracking east through Kentucky during the late night and early part of Monday. The system occluded over the state and large snowfall amounts were recorded north and west of Dayton. Drier air in the occlusion was pulled into central Ohio, limiting snowfall totals significantly in this area. Much of the early part of the event started as rain in the southern portion of the CWA and snow totals were notably lower in southern Ohio, southeast Indiana, and northern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer located 2 miles north-northeast of Logan measured 4.8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Sunday and Monday March 24th and 25th, snow and sleet along with some rain spread through the region, with significant snow found over metropolitan Dayton and to the northwest. Surface low pressure moved from the Tennessee Valley to Ohio during the overnight. This low got added influence with an upper low tracking east through Kentucky during the late night and early part of Monday. The system occluded over the state and large snowfall amounts were recorded north and west of Dayton. Drier air in the occlusion was pulled into central Ohio, limiting snowfall totals significantly in this area. Much of the early part of the event started as rain in the southern portion of the CWA and snow totals were notably lower in southern Ohio, southeast Indiana, and northern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter on the northeast side of Troy measured 4.7 inches of snow, while four inches of snow was measured by the highway department. Social media showed 4 inches of snow in Piqua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Influenced by a highly unstable, but strongly capped atmosphere ahead of a frontal boundary moving out of Illinois, the combination of 3000 to 4000 j/kg surface based CAPE, mid-level lapse rates between 8 and 9 C/km and 0-6 km deep layer shear of 35 to 55 knots set the stage for rapid supercellular development. The convection transitioned to clusters of bowing segments as storms congealed producing an increased risk for high end damaging wind. Broad, but strong rotation established quickly in the initial storms, allowing for rapid production of hail, some exceeding gold ball size. Numerous funnel clouds were reported in the early stages of the event, indicating conditions were favorable for tornadic development. However, the development of tornadoes was severely limited due to just enough mixed layer inhibition in the lowest levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn was destroyed at this location, however several of the timbers were rotten. There was damage to trees on the property as well. The damage was likely the results of a circulation that had touched down just to the southwest of this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sagged south into northern Ohio causing showers and thunderstorms to develop.  A few of the stronger thunderstorms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather developed across north central and northeast Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of June 25th.  Ohio was located on the periphery of high pressure over the Southeastern states.  Moisture increased from the southwest around the ridge with dewpoints increasing to near 70 degrees.  An unstable airmass developed during the afternoon as temperatures rose into the mid 80s with mixed layer CAPE values near 2000 j/kg.  Energy moving through the flow aloft helped to initiate convection and provided the shear necessary for damaging winds and large hail.||Numerous tress were brought down by strong winds across northeast Ohio.  Portions of Lorain and Cuyahoga counties were hit particularly bad with downed trees and power outages with several trees falling on homes and garages.  First Energy reported 29,000 customers without power in Lorain and Cuyahoga Counties with at least 10,000 more customers without power in northeast Ohio.  A man was also seriously injured while driving on Interstate 480 when a sign fell off the Valley View bridge and hit his car.  The north side of Canton was also hit particularly hard with numerous reports of downed trees, damage to a grocery store, and a picnic shelter destroyed at Nimishillen Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The media reported numerous trees down in Jefferson and Sheriff's office confirmed trees and powerlines down across the county.  First Energy reported 5000 customers lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front and upper level trough pushed across the Upper Ohio Valley on July 10th producing widespread severe weather and flash flooding from eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. This included 3 separate tornadoes. One tornado occurred in Lawrence county, PA, with 2 additional tornadoes across portions of Tuscarawas and Carroll counties in Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported numerous trees down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A circulation touched down on the west side of Willshire, along State Road 81 near the Indiana/Ohio State Line.  The tornado traveled southeast down Green Street to the intersection of Green Street and State Route 49.  An eyewitness observed the funnel make contact with the ground in his yard, snap off two large trees and continue down the street.  The survey revealed additional tree damage along the path.  Another eyewitness observed condensation vortices being injected into the funnel as his garage was rolled off its foundation.  A portion of a house roof was removed and thrown over a neighbor���s home.  A total of 13 homes suffered varying degrees of damage.  The circulation was on the ground less than 3 minutes with a maximum width of 80 yards and maximum wind speeds around 85 mph.  The same storm continued into Mercer County and later produced another weak tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eyewitness reports and a National Weather Service Damage survey indicate a circulation quickly formed and touched down on the leading edge of a larger area of downburst winds along County Road Y, west of County Road 12A.  The tornado caused some minor roof damage to a residence and snapped or uprooted several trees in the immediate area (DI: TH DOD: 3); some debarking of tree tops was observed.  Wet ground likely aided uprooting of trees.  Shortly after touchdown, the tornado became outflow dominant and quickly dissipated with debris being carried by straight line winds to the east.  Maximum path length was 50 yards with a maximum width of 25 yards.  Wind speeds were estimated at around 85 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey of damage at a farm indicated a combination of the remnant circulation of a tornado to the southwest of the site, as well as an anchoring failure, resulted in the collapse of a grain leg collapse and damage to several empty grain bins.  The grain leg was anchored by a three point system, with the northeast side of the leg being unanchored.  As the strong winds impacted an anchor on the southwest side, the leg began to buckle and shift, causing the bending of a large I-beam and the uplift of a cement block where an anchor was set to the northwest of the leg.  The grain leg twisted and collapsed onto several bins.  Given the failure of the anchoring, winds are estimated at around 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through the region during the late afternoon and early evening hours. Strong shear, instability and steep mid-level lapse rates all combined with the wave to produce numerous thunderstorms across Illinois. These storms moved across northern Indiana and slowly started weakening as they entered northwestern Ohio. A few of the storms still produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported a tree was blown down, bringing down an electrical line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and unstable air-mass advected into the region.  Outflow from morning convection and some mid/high clouds helped to keep the area capped for most of the day.  Convection began firing over Iowa around 18 UTC and quickly formed into a forward propagating Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) that followed the instability gradient into northwestern Ohio during the late evening hours. The storms had weakened considerably moving out of northern Indiana, with an isolated report of wind damage in Van Wert County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported power lines down in the Wren area from strong winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm survey indicated that a circulation rapidly touched down at a residence on the south side of County Road J.   A detached garage suffered extensive damage, as well as an adjacent barn suffering total loss of its roof (DI: SBO, DOD: 4).  The tornado continued northeast with debris from the residence impacting a two story home, causing the destruction of a porch, as well as a large hole punched into the west side of the upper story of the house (DI: FR12, DOD: 4).  A large pine tree had the top half snapped off and then thrown roughly 50 feet to rest against other trees on the property.  The tornado continued across a field into a small grove of trees, where the tornado likely became outflow dominant and dissipated.  While damage to the second residence could indicate much stronger wind speeds, a maximum wind speed of 95 mph was estimated due to the impacts of larger debris from the point of touchdown.  The maximum width of the tornado was around 75 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather developed across north central and northeast Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of June 25th.  Ohio was located on the periphery of high pressure over the Southeastern states.  Moisture increased from the southwest around the ridge with dewpoints increasing to near 70 degrees.  An unstable airmass developed during the afternoon as temperatures rose into the mid 80s with mixed layer CAPE values near 2000 j/kg.  Energy moving through the flow aloft helped to initiate convection and provided the shear necessary for damaging winds and large hail.||Numerous tress were brought down by strong winds across northeast Ohio.  Portions of Lorain and Cuyahoga counties were hit particularly bad with downed trees and power outages with several trees falling on homes and garages.  First Energy reported 29,000 customers without power in Lorain and Cuyahoga Counties with at least 10,000 more customers without power in northeast Ohio.  A man was also seriously injured while driving on Interstate 480 when a sign fell off the Valley View bridge and hit his car.  The north side of Canton was also hit particularly hard with numerous reports of downed trees, damage to a grocery store, and a picnic shelter destroyed at Nimishillen Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple campers blown over by strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of convection moved across portions of central Indiana and Ohio during the late evening hours of June 12, lasting into the early morning hours of June 13. The main threats from these storms were damaging winds and isolated weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado briefly touched down to the West of River Train Road. Ten percent of a large aluminum barn was removed and had four garage doors blown out. Some of the debris from this building caused collateral impact damage to surrounding structures on both north and south facing aspects. A large dent occurred near the top of the southwest side of a grain silo. Another aluminum building incurred siding damage and a second story window was blown in from the south. This property was surrounded by farmland and no additional damage was noted in the area. Based on the damage surveyed, the maximum wind speed from this tornado was estimated to be 75 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of convection moved across portions of central Indiana and Ohio during the late evening hours of June 12, lasting into the early morning hours of June 13. The main threats from these storms were damaging winds and isolated weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down on the north side of State Route 219 east of New Knoxville. The tornado tracked east-southeast where minor damage was done to the north side of the administrative building at the Neil Armstrong Airport. A large amount of sheet metal and tree limbs were deposited on the runway. Further along the path, the tornado damaged six houses and three barns and uprooted trees. One barn was 80 percent destroyed. At the end of the tornado track, a double wide mobile home was picked up and tossed 100 feet to the north on its side. Based on the damage surveyed, the maximum wind speed from this tornado was estimated to be 75 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Influenced by a highly unstable, but strongly capped atmosphere ahead of a frontal boundary moving out of Illinois, the combination of 3000 to 4000 j/kg surface based CAPE, mid-level lapse rates between 8 and 9 C/km and 0-6 km deep layer shear of 35 to 55 knots set the stage for rapid supercellular development. The convection transitioned to clusters of bowing segments as storms congealed producing an increased risk for high end damaging wind. Broad, but strong rotation established quickly in the initial storms, allowing for rapid production of hail, some exceeding gold ball size. Numerous funnel clouds were reported in the early stages of the event, indicating conditions were favorable for tornadic development. However, the development of tornadoes was severely limited due to just enough mixed layer inhibition in the lowest levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm survey of damage indicated that two weak and brief tornado occurred, separated by a quarter mile. A convergent, variable track pattern was noted in a wheat field. While the wheat was laid down, actual damage was minimal. The maximum width was 15 yards, with maximum winds of 70 mph.

Fishers Grocery store was damaged and a picnic shelter was completely destroyed at Nimishillen Park.

In the muggy summer environment, a complex of showers and thunderstorms formed during the evening hours in central Ohio.  The individual cells moved southeast.  Repetitive showers hit a small portion of eastern Perry County and western Morgan County.||Maximum rain estimates were on the order of 3 to 3.5 inches in around 4 hours from 2200E on the 8th through about 0200E on the 9th.   Measured amounts from 2 gauges around New Lexington had 2.2 inches of rain.||A new round of convection formed during the mid and late afternoon and moved into southeastern Ohio after 1600E.  This caused a second round of high water.  The combined effects brought Duck Creek and the Little Muskingum River in Washington County over its banks.   The second round also helped cause the lower reaches of Sunday Creek to cause some minor flooding in Athens County.   ||One unofficial measurement from a spotter located 4 miles northwest of Corning had a 30 hour total of 6.25 inches of rain from the afternoon of the 8th into the evening on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small stream flooding occurred. ||The rainfall maximum affected the headwaters of Sunday Creek and Moxahala Creek. The water from Sunday Creek drained south through Corning, causing a dozen evacuations in town.  The water was 4 feet over Route 13 in Corning.  The water from Moxahala Creek flowed north and northeast.  Its waters rose rapidly and flooded portions of Crooksville.  Around 16 units of an apartment complex called Village Green was flooded.  Water was as much as 3 feet deep.  Also in Crooksville, a mobile home park and some homes along China Street were flooded.   Around 15 people were evacuated in Crooksville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Influenced by a highly unstable, but strongly capped atmosphere ahead of a frontal boundary moving out of Illinois, the combination of 3000 to 4000 j/kg surface based CAPE, mid-level lapse rates between 8 and 9 C/km and 0-6 km deep layer shear of 35 to 55 knots set the stage for rapid supercellular development. The convection transitioned to clusters of bowing segments as storms congealed producing an increased risk for high end damaging wind. Broad, but strong rotation established quickly in the initial storms, allowing for rapid production of hail, some exceeding gold ball size. Numerous funnel clouds were reported in the early stages of the event, indicating conditions were favorable for tornadic development. However, the development of tornadoes was severely limited due to just enough mixed layer inhibition in the lowest levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported numerous trees and power lines down from North Creek to Leipsic. A mobile home was overturned on the south side of North Creek, with one person suffering minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well ahead of a cold front, showers and thunderstorms formed in Ohio during the heat of the afternoon and moved east.  Short lines of showers and thunderstorms formed parallel to the flow causing some repetitive showers over portions of Morgan County between 1730 and 1900E on the 23rd.  Another round of showers affected the area around 2000E.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over wet terrain, rains estimated at 2 to 3 inches fell in a few separate rounds of convection from the McConnelsville vicinity on north and east in Morgan County.  Small streams flooded roads.  For example, Four Mile Run flooded in Meigsville Township.  In Bristol Township, County Route 62 called Mummey Road was flooded.  In the Meigs Creek basin, portions of County Route 11 were flooded.  Bell Creek flooded yards around McConnelsville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported numerous roads closed with some completely impassable due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather developed across north central and northeast Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of June 25th.  Ohio was located on the periphery of high pressure over the Southeastern states.  Moisture increased from the southwest around the ridge with dewpoints increasing to near 70 degrees.  An unstable airmass developed during the afternoon as temperatures rose into the mid 80s with mixed layer CAPE values near 2000 j/kg.  Energy moving through the flow aloft helped to initiate convection and provided the shear necessary for damaging winds and large hail.||Numerous tress were brought down by strong winds across northeast Ohio.  Portions of Lorain and Cuyahoga counties were hit particularly bad with downed trees and power outages with several trees falling on homes and garages.  First Energy reported 29,000 customers without power in Lorain and Cuyahoga Counties with at least 10,000 more customers without power in northeast Ohio.  A man was also seriously injured while driving on Interstate 480 when a sign fell off the Valley View bridge and hit his car.  The north side of Canton was also hit particularly hard with numerous reports of downed trees, damage to a grocery store, and a picnic shelter destroyed at Nimishillen Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Police and ODOT reported penny size hail in Millersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across northwest Ohio during the evening on June 23rd as instability increased across the area.  Dry air in the mid-levels contributed to a threat for strong downburst winds.  A few of the thunderstorms became severe with damaging wind gusts across Lucas and Wood Counties.  Numerous trees and tree limbs were downed across Maumee and Bowling Green with two trees falling on a house south of Portage in Wood County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The media relayed reports of downed trees and limbs in Maumee with power lines causing road closures to Ford Street and through the downtown area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a result of morning showers and clouds, a significant temperature contrast existed west to east across Ohio by mid afternoon.  Readings were in the mid 80s in western Ohio, but only in the 70s in eastern counties.  Small thunderstorm clusters formed in northern Ohio late in the day and moved southeast.  One cluster was oriented along the flow, resulting in repetitive rains over a portion of Morgan County during the evening.  After a brief lull, another storm affected the same area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automatic gauge near McConnelsville reported 2.2 inches of rain in 2 to 3 hours between 1800 and 2100E.  Another .5 inches of rain fell between 2200E and 2300E.   Rain estimates of over 3 inches fell around Joetown.  ||Small streams flooded roads.  One example was in the Millers Run basin, where County Route 56 was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Influenced by a highly unstable, but strongly capped atmosphere ahead of a frontal boundary moving out of Illinois, the combination of 3000 to 4000 j/kg surface based CAPE, mid-level lapse rates between 8 and 9 C/km and 0-6 km deep layer shear of 35 to 55 knots set the stage for rapid supercellular development. The convection transitioned to clusters of bowing segments as storms congealed producing an increased risk for high end damaging wind. Broad, but strong rotation established quickly in the initial storms, allowing for rapid production of hail, some exceeding gold ball size. Numerous funnel clouds were reported in the early stages of the event, indicating conditions were favorable for tornadic development. However, the development of tornadoes was severely limited due to just enough mixed layer inhibition in the lowest levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS survey of damage north of Hamler indicated a circulation quickly intensified and touched down onto a barn, destroying it and throwing the debris to the northeast for nearly a half mile (DI: SBO DI: 6). Several large trees in the immediate area were snapped. An adjacent soybean field indicated a convergent wind pattern which continued towards a small grove of trees where the tornado dissipated. The maximum width was 50 yards with maximum wind speeds estimated at 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system was centered over northern Ohio. A more fall like pattern of cool air aloft was in place, setting the stage for the development of showers and a few thunderstorms. Limited shear and instability kept most convection well below severe limits, with reports of cold air funnel clouds common given weak surface flow and cold air aloft. However, three weaker thunderstorms did congeal along an outflow boundary in northern Defiance County, causing rapid intensification and eventual collapse of the resultant storm. Radar data and visual observations showed the storm briefly exhibited mini supercell type structure with a well-defined wall cloud and funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees suffered limb damage on The Bend Road, just north of Beerbower Road. Metal sheeting on the property was blown a quarter mile southwest to the next damage location, where it may have impacted other objects.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves diving through a broad upper level trough brought rounds of showers and thunderstorms through the last several days of June. With a weakly capped atmosphere, a cold pool associated with two such upper level waves helped to spark two areas of convection in the afternoon on June 28th. There were numerous reports of wind damage across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. In addition, training thunderstorms produced several areas of flooding, including some flash flooding across several counties in western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong frontal system remained nearly stationary across the Upper Ohio Valley during the evening hours of June 12th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across northern Ohio early in the evening. The storms became more numerous as the evening progressed. Many of the storms became severe. Dozens of reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Scattered power outages resulted from these storms.||A second round of strong thunderstorms occurred after midnight.  A long lived bowing line of storms or Derecho developed to the west of the area.  This line crossed the I-75 corridor just after midnight and then raced east across the remainder of the northern Ohio during the early morning hours.  Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph occurred across most of the area resulting in considerable damage and scattered power outages.  A weak tornado also occurred in Wood County as the line moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds uprooted a large tree and broke several tree limbs.
