EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance helped to enhance showers and thunderstorms across central Ohio during the evening of July 8 and into the early morning hours of July 9. Some of these storms became strong to severe with damaging winds and sub severe hail the primary threats. Later in the evening these storms continued to move slowly over the same areas, increasing the threat for heavy rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 3 feet of rushing water was reported in some locations near Cridersville due to heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong frontal system remained nearly stationary across the Upper Ohio Valley during the evening hours of June 12th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across northern Ohio early in the evening. The storms became more numerous as the evening progressed. Many of the storms became severe. Dozens of reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Scattered power outages resulted from these storms.||A second round of strong thunderstorms occurred after midnight.  A long lived bowing line of storms or Derecho developed to the west of the area.  This line crossed the I-75 corridor just after midnight and then raced east across the remainder of the northern Ohio during the early morning hours.  Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph occurred across most of the area resulting in considerable damage and scattered power outages.  A weak tornado also occurred in Wood County as the line moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 66 mph thunderstorm wind gust was measured by an automated sensor at Fostoria High School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon storms developed in a highly unstable and humid airmass over Ohio on the 9th. High rainfall rates and saturated ground cover supported flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two and a half inches of rain fell over saturated ground in Mansfield Ohio on the evening of the 8th and into the early morning hours of the 9th. Most of the flooding that occurred in Mansfield was caused by the excessive runoff and poor drainage. The fire department evacuated homes using boats along 5th Street in downtown. |Flooding on Park Avenue East put roads and railroad tracks underwater. Some cars were submerged. On Mulberry Street, a few inches of water inundated the first floor of some businesses.  The rain caused the already swollen Rocky Fork, a tributary to the Mohican River, to come out of its banks in the northern part of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon storms developed in a highly unstable and humid airmass over Ohio on the 9th. High rainfall rates and saturated ground cover supported flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was reported in Tully Township and Martel as heavy rainfall estimated between two and three inches fell over the areas on the night of the 9th and early morning hours of the 10th. Numerous washouts occurred due to excessive runoff and flowing water from tributaries of the Olentangy River. Access into Tully was cut off for the better part of the next day. The flooding occurred in areas that do not usually flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon storms developed in a highly unstable and humid airmass over Ohio on the 9th. High rainfall rates and saturated ground cover supported flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tributary to the Clear Fork of the Mohican River rose rapidly during the overnight hours of the 9th to the 10th of July in Ontario Ohio. An estimated 2.5 fell in during the evening hours of the 9th, falling over saturated ground. The three day rainfall leading up to this event was close to 8, so conditions were ripe for maximum runoff. A resident along Rock Road awoke in the early morning hours to water filling her home. They were trapped in the home with 6 feet of water in the first floor before the water receded almost as quickly as it rose. The residents lost their belongings and sustained significant damage to their home, but there were fortunately no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon storms developed in a highly unstable and humid airmass over Ohio on the 9th. High rainfall rates and saturated ground cover supported flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree and several large limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather developed across north central and northeast Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of June 25th.  Ohio was located on the periphery of high pressure over the Southeastern states.  Moisture increased from the southwest around the ridge with dewpoints increasing to near 70 degrees.  An unstable airmass developed during the afternoon as temperatures rose into the mid 80s with mixed layer CAPE values near 2000 j/kg.  Energy moving through the flow aloft helped to initiate convection and provided the shear necessary for damaging winds and large hail.||Numerous tress were brought down by strong winds across northeast Ohio.  Portions of Lorain and Cuyahoga counties were hit particularly bad with downed trees and power outages with several trees falling on homes and garages.  First Energy reported 29,000 customers without power in Lorain and Cuyahoga Counties with at least 10,000 more customers without power in northeast Ohio.  A man was also seriously injured while driving on Interstate 480 when a sign fell off the Valley View bridge and hit his car.  The north side of Canton was also hit particularly hard with numerous reports of downed trees, damage to a grocery store, and a picnic shelter destroyed at Nimishillen Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The media reported trees and powerlines down in North Ridgeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front and upper level trough pushed across the Upper Ohio Valley on July 10th producing widespread severe weather and flash flooding from eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. This included 3 separate tornadoes. One tornado occurred in Lawrence county, PA, with 2 additional tornadoes across portions of Tuscarawas and Carroll counties in Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of a tornado that originally touched down in Tuscarawas county and crossed into Carroll county near Atwood Lake. See Storm Data for Tuscarawas county Ohio for details on this tornado. This tornado produced EF-0 to EF-1 damage from south of Bolivar to near Atwood Lake, before crossing into Carroll county near Atwood Lake, and producing EF-0 damage to trees before dissipating south of Delroy. Maximum winds with this tornado were estimated to be from 90 to 100 MPH, with a path length  near 14 miles. The maximum path width was near 300 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front and upper level trough pushed across the Upper Ohio Valley on July 10th producing widespread severe weather and flash flooding from eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. This included 3 separate tornadoes. One tornado occurred in Lawrence county, PA, with 2 additional tornadoes across portions of Tuscarawas and Carroll counties in Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey found an EF-1 tornado touched down in Tuscarawas county south of Bolivar and continued into Carroll county near Atwood Lake, before dissipating. EF-0 damage was found along SR 212 south of Bolivar where numerous large limbs were snapped and trees uprooted northwest of Zoar. EF-0 damage became more significant as the  circulation descended the lee side of a hill west of Mineral City, where a broad swath of snapped trees was observed. EF-1 structural damage was noted in Mineral City where the roof of the fire house was lifted and cinder block  walls collapsed. Several homes lost shingles, and windows were blown out on northwest facing walls. Thereafter damage was confined primarily to trees before the tornado dissipated. Maximum winds Were estimated to be from 90 to 100 MPH, with a path length  near 14 miles. The maximum path width was near 300 yards. |See Storm Data for Carroll county, Ohio for the remainder of this tornado path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front and upper level trough pushed across the Upper Ohio Valley on July 10th producing widespread severe weather and flash flooding from eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. This included 3 separate tornadoes. One tornado occurred in Lawrence county, PA, with 2 additional tornadoes across portions of Tuscarawas and Carroll counties in Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey found an EF-1 tornado that touched down northwest of Kilgore and dissipated southwest of Amsterdam in Carroll county. Damage was first noted along Route 332 south of Petersburgh, OH where trees were downed or snapped off. This damage continued southeast along Aster Road, with low end EF-1 damage to hardwood trees. Tree damage continued along Raindrop Road and Pronto Road, with high end EF-1 damage to trees along Pronto road. Damage then rapidly ended near Cavalry Road as the tornado dissipated. Maximum winds were estimated to be from 100 to 110 MPH, with a path length near 9 miles. The maximum path width was 150 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon storms developed in a highly unstable and humid airmass over Ohio on the 9th. High rainfall rates and saturated ground cover supported flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads in Morrow County were closed due to high water. A swath of 2 to upwards of 3.5 inches of rain fell over portions of central Morrow County the night of the 8th. The rain fell over already saturated soil and the rivers and creeks were running high prior to the onset of new rain. By daybreak in Mt. Gilead 75 people were evacuated from apartments and 3 from trailers as flood waters inundated their property. In total 20 properties saw water inundate the first floor with a few inches of water. The fairgrounds flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon storms developed in a highly unstable and humid airmass over Ohio on the 9th. High rainfall rates and saturated ground cover supported flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flood was occurring across the county with the worst conditions in Crestline after an estimated four inches of rain fell in 4 hours.  Six homes and one business along Front Street had flood waters into the first floor. Several Streets were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon storms developed in a highly unstable and humid airmass over Ohio on the 9th. High rainfall rates and saturated ground cover supported flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After 3 to 4 inches of rain fell over the Black Fork of the Mohican River on the evening of the 8th, it pushed the river out of its banks in the town of Shelby. The town was cut off from east to west. Main Street was closed and the Fire Department and several businesses were evacuated. Numerous homes sustained water damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon storms developed in a highly unstable and humid airmass over Ohio on the 9th. High rainfall rates and saturated ground cover supported flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding was reported in eastern Crawford County. In Galion, the water inundated Heise Park as the Olentangy River tributaries were overwhelmed. Ohio 309 from North East Street at Chalres Street to Harding Way East was inundated and closed.  A house on Olentangy Court was inundated in the first floor. In Bucyrus, it was the Sandusky River that came out of its banks flooding several basements. A vehicle was damaged as the driver entered flooded Knauss Road. North Sandusky Ave, River Street, Temple Road, and Bethel Roads were closed. All of this flooding was the result of 2 to 4 inches of rainfall from the evening of the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in an unstable air mass ahead of a cold front during the afternoon. These storms began to organize and became severe. The main threat from these storms was damaging wind and large hail. Some locations were hit by multiple storms over a short period of time. This caused very heavy rainfall that resulted in flooding and flash flooding.

A large, slow moving area of rain with embedded heavier showers moved across the area from the late morning into the afternoon. The result was heavy rainfall with localized flooding and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Evacuations of a building were occurring due to high water caused by heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather developed across north central and northeast Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of June 25th.  Ohio was located on the periphery of high pressure over the Southeastern states.  Moisture increased from the southwest around the ridge with dewpoints increasing to near 70 degrees.  An unstable airmass developed during the afternoon as temperatures rose into the mid 80s with mixed layer CAPE values near 2000 j/kg.  Energy moving through the flow aloft helped to initiate convection and provided the shear necessary for damaging winds and large hail.||Numerous tress were brought down by strong winds across northeast Ohio.  Portions of Lorain and Cuyahoga counties were hit particularly bad with downed trees and power outages with several trees falling on homes and garages.  First Energy reported 29,000 customers without power in Lorain and Cuyahoga Counties with at least 10,000 more customers without power in northeast Ohio.  A man was also seriously injured while driving on Interstate 480 when a sign fell off the Valley View bridge and hit his car.  The north side of Canton was also hit particularly hard with numerous reports of downed trees, damage to a grocery store, and a picnic shelter destroyed at Nimishillen Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported two large trees blown down in Solon, one of which was two feet in diameter.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a large sign blew off the Valley View Bridge and hit a car traveling on Interstate 480.  The man was seriously injured from the accident.  Trees were also down in Garfield Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance helped to enhance showers and thunderstorms across central Ohio during the evening of July 8 and into the early morning hours of July 9. Some of these storms became strong to severe with damaging winds and sub severe hail the primary threats. Later in the evening these storms continued to move slowly over the same areas, increasing the threat for heavy rainfall and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of and with a cold front moving across the upper Ohio Valley, with some severe storms across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. A brief tornado touched down near the border of Carroll and Columbiana counties, and a stronger downburst occurred in East Liverpool in Columbiana county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey found an EF-0 tornado crossed into Columbiana county from Carroll county west of Summitville near Acker Road, and then dissipated shortly thereafter. See Storm Data for Carroll county, for the beginning of this tornado. The tornado damaged trees in its path for nearly one mile into Columbiana before dissipating. The entire path length of this storm was 3.2 miles, with a maximum path width of 75 yards. Maximum winds were estimated at 75 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South of a cold front, mid level support and upper level jet dynamics helped form and enhance showers and thunderstorms in eastern Ohio during the late evening on the 12th.  These clusters trained across portions of Vinton, Athens, and Washington Counties.  In this maximum rainfall band, amounts from about 2300E on the 12th to 0300E on the 13th were on the order of 2 to 4 inches.  The maximum rainfall reported was from the cooperative observer along the Ohio River at Newport.  They measured 4.74 inches of rain.   The Marietta cooperative observer measured 4 inches, while a nearby automatic gauge recorded 3.5 inches of rain.   In Marietta, it was the heaviest 24 hour rainfall amount since the remnants of Hurricane Ivan in September 2004. ||The emergency managers and 911 centers from Vinton and Athens Counties reported no serious problems.  The maximum rainfall in Athens County, fell east and south of the town of Athens.  Some volunteer spotters measured over 3 inches.  In Vinton County the rainfall maximum was on the order of 2 inches. ||Further east, significant flash flooding occurred across southern Washington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding occurred across southern Washington County during the predawn hours.  Widespread street flooding occurred in Belpre and Marietta with basement flooding.  One resident reported 2 feet of water in their basement. ||Near the mouth of Mill Branch with the Little Hocking River, vehicles were flooded and water got into several homes.  Also along the Little Hocking River, 2 cars were submerged along Oxbow Road.  The water was 2 to 4 feet deep in spots.  An 18 year old female from 1 of the vehicles made it safely out, but her truck was submerged in a soy bean field off the road.  Minor flooding occurred in a school west of Fleming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple showers and thunderstorms developed and moved across the same locations during the late afternoon into the evening. As a result, some flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was estimated to be 3 feet deep on North Hamilton Road near Warner Road due to heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving showers and thunderstorms developed around a low pressure area during the daytime heating. Some of these storms moved over the same areas repeatedly, causing heavy rainfall and flash flooding to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water from runoff due to heavy rainfall was reported to make a few roads in the area impassible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a stalled frontal boundary draped across Lake Erie and the upper Ohio Valley.  Locally heavy rainfall was reported across northern Ohio with reports of up 6 inches in Lake County.  A tornado touched down in eastern Cuyahoga County and caused some damage at a small college.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed two trees.  A farm wagon was also blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary boundary remained the focus for afternoon showers and thunderstorms again on June 30th. While shear and instability remained meager, with cape values of 1000j/kg or less, some storms were able to support themselves to severe levels. A little more than a dozen wind damage and hail reports combined made up the afternoon. But, once again, training thunderstorms produced flooding problems, this time over Allegheny county in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported trees and large limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of convection moved across portions of central Indiana and Ohio during the late evening hours of June 12, lasting into the early morning hours of June 13. The main threats from these storms were damaging winds and isolated weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles were snapped on Route 364 due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system was centered over northern Ohio. A more fall like pattern of cool air aloft was in place, setting the stage for the development of showers and a few thunderstorms. Limited shear and instability kept most convection well below severe limits, with reports of cold air funnel clouds common given weak surface flow and cold air aloft. However, three weaker thunderstorms did congeal along an outflow boundary in northern Defiance County, causing rapid intensification and eventual collapse of the resultant storm. Radar data and visual observations showed the storm briefly exhibited mini supercell type structure with a well-defined wall cloud and funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several small tree limbs were reported down across the town of Ney. A roughly six foot wide by 5 foot deep edge of a corn field on the southwest side of Ney was flattened. A wooden swing set next to the field was thrown into the field and destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong frontal system remained nearly stationary across the Upper Ohio Valley during the evening hours of June 12th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across northern Ohio early in the evening. The storms became more numerous as the evening progressed. Many of the storms became severe. Dozens of reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Scattered power outages resulted from these storms.||A second round of strong thunderstorms occurred after midnight.  A long lived bowing line of storms or Derecho developed to the west of the area.  This line crossed the I-75 corridor just after midnight and then raced east across the remainder of the northern Ohio during the early morning hours.  Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph occurred across most of the area resulting in considerable damage and scattered power outages.  A weak tornado also occurred in Wood County as the line moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees and power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey found several trees uprooted along St Andrews Drive.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of snapped trees and limbs were found during an NWS storm survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon storms developed in a highly unstable and humid airmass over Ohio on the 9th. High rainfall rates and saturated ground cover supported flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the Knox County EMA, Fredericktown and Danville were hit the hardest in that area after an estimated 2 to 4 inches of rain fell on the evening of the 8th. A campground on the Kokosing River was inundated.  Knox County flooded roads include S.R. 205, S.R. 95 north of Tom's Road, and Knox Lake Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of convection moved across portions of central Indiana and Ohio during the late evening hours of June 12, lasting into the early morning hours of June 13. The main threats from these storms were damaging winds and isolated weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi trailer was overturned at Walmart west of Interstate 75 in Wapakoneta due to thunderstorm winds. Trees were also reported down in the city of Wapakoneta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather developed across north central and northeast Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of June 25th.  Ohio was located on the periphery of high pressure over the Southeastern states.  Moisture increased from the southwest around the ridge with dewpoints increasing to near 70 degrees.  An unstable airmass developed during the afternoon as temperatures rose into the mid 80s with mixed layer CAPE values near 2000 j/kg.  Energy moving through the flow aloft helped to initiate convection and provided the shear necessary for damaging winds and large hail.||Numerous tress were brought down by strong winds across northeast Ohio.  Portions of Lorain and Cuyahoga counties were hit particularly bad with downed trees and power outages with several trees falling on homes and garages.  First Energy reported 29,000 customers without power in Lorain and Cuyahoga Counties with at least 10,000 more customers without power in northeast Ohio.  A man was also seriously injured while driving on Interstate 480 when a sign fell off the Valley View bridge and hit his car.  The north side of Canton was also hit particularly hard with numerous reports of downed trees, damage to a grocery store, and a picnic shelter destroyed at Nimishillen Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported large tree limbs down in Chesterland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms resulted in heavy rain and flash flooding across portions of west central Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of high water due to heavy rainfall in and around Bellefontaine.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water due to heavy rainfall was reported over several roads between Fort Recovery and Wabash.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rushing water over many roads was occurring from around Rushylvania to Big Springs due to heavy rainfall.

A strong low pressure system lifting northeast through the Midwest and Great Lakes brought a period of moderate freezing rain to northwest Ohio on February 26th. Ice accretions near a tenth of an inch were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate rain overspread the region on February 26th. Surface temperatures hovered near freezing during the morning and early afternoon hours which allowed the rain to freeze on mainly elevated surfaces. Ice accretions near a tenth of an inch were common on trees, power lines, bridges, and overpasses. This combined with wind gusts to 30 mph aided in downing a few tree limbs. There were also reports of isolated power outages and accidents across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prefrontal band of fast moving showers and thunderstorms trained over extreme southeast Ohio from 0630E through 1300E.  The maximum observed rainfall was on the order of an inch in 3 hours, with 1.25 to 1.75 inches in 6 hours.   The maximum amount measured was 1.73 inches in Kitts Hill in about 6 hours.  ||With the ground wet from previous rain and snow melt, plus the lack of spring vegetation, some streams overflowed, causing minor flooding.  Water blocked and closed roads. No evacuations or damage to structures were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams flooded and blocked roads in scattered locations in much of the county.  For example, Solida Creek flooded near South Point.   Hog Run flooded near Ironton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold north to northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the afternoon hours of February 16th.  The snow intensified during the evening hours with accumulations of 1 to 2 inches per hour.  Visibilities at times were less than a quarter of a mile.  The snow showers dissipated during the early morning hours of the 17th.  A swath of 6 to 9 inches of snow was reported in Lorain County.  6 to 9 inches of snow was also reported across northern Geauga County.  In Lorain County, spotters in Oberlin reported 8.2 and 9.0 inches of snow.  A peak total of 9.0 inches was reported at Chardon in Geauga County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold north to northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the afternoon hours of February 16th.  The snow intensified during the evening hours with accumulations of 1 to 2 inches per hour.  Visibilities at times were less than a quarter of a mile.  The snow showers dissipated during the early morning hours of the 17th.  6 to 9 inches of snow was also reported across northern Geauga County.   A peak total of 9.0 inches was reported at Chardon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and a fresh snow pack allowed for the formation of fog across northwestern Ohio. Pockets of dense fog occurred with some locations reporting near zero visibilities. The fog played a factor in a 14 vehicle pile up on U.S. 24 in Paulding county when a SUV pulled out in front of a tanker carrying ethanol fuel. The driver of the SUV was pronounced dead at the scene. The accident resulted in 12 additional vehicles becoming involved in the scene as well as the rupture and release of 1,500 gallons of ethanol fuel into the median of U.S. 24. The highway was closed for several hours due to the clean up of both vehicles and the spilled fuel. Nine non-life-threatening injuries also resulted from the accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 14 vehicles were involved in a morning pile-up on US-24 near County Road 87 in Paulding County.  Ohio State Highway Patrol troopers reported that a northbound SUV on County Road 87 failed to yield at the US-24 intersection.  The vehicle, driven by a 28 year old female, was struck by a tanker truck carrying ethanol fuel.  The tanker ruptured with approximately 1,500 gallons of the fuel leaking into the median.  Dense fog blanketed the area with the troopers reporting visibilities of around 100 feet.  The accident then lead to further vehicles becoming involved as drivers tried to avoid the accident.  A total of 11 commercial vehicles (many being semi-trailers), two passenger cars and a pickup hauling excavating equipment were involved.  Nine other people were injured in the pile-up, all suffering non-life-threatening injuries.  No specifics were available on ages or injuries.  The fog lifted around 9 AM local time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north, through southern Ohio, during the morning of the 12th.  Heating and instability increased during the afternoon with dew points reaching the lower 70s during the afternoon.  Disturbances in the mid and upper levels rippled southeast initially.  Addditional disturbances then moved east overnight Wednesday night.||Showers and thunderstorms formed over northern Ohio early on the evening of the 12th, and streaked southeast.   The southern edge of this convection finally sank down into portions of southeast Ohio late at night.   A few storms were severe, causing wind damage. ||Meanwhile, a separate large cluster of showers and thunderstorms moved out of northern Indiana into southeast Ohio during the predawn hours.  The southern edge of that convection became oriented west to east along the fast flow aloft.  Training of showers did cause 1.5 to 2 inches of rain across portions of Jackson, Vinton, Athens, Meigs and northern Gallia Counties.   Due to the relatively dry ground, no serious flash flooding occurred.  Some street flooding was observed in towns such as Athens and Gallipolis.  ||The final round of convection, associated with the cold front, streaked through southeast Ohio during the late morning hours on the 13th.  This caused another brief downpour with pea size hail and wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph.  No significant damage occurred.  Jackson Ohio reported about .9 inches in just over an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved north across western Ohio on March 18th.  A warm front stretching east from the low also lifted north across the region.  Rain developed along this front.  Temperatures at the onset of the precipitation remained below freezing.  A four to six hour period of freezing rain occurred in the Youngstown-Warren area before temperatures warmed.  Further north the freezing precipitation lasted for a shorter duration.  Up to a third of an inch of ice accumulation was reported in Mahoning and Trumbull Counties with around a quarter inch in Portage and Ashtabula Counties.  The ice accumulated mainly on trees and grassy surfaces.  Strong southeast winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts as high as 35 mph reported.  Some trees and limbs were toppled by the ice and there were also a few reports of power outages.  A few accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A four to six hour period of freezing rain occurred in Portage County.  Between a quarter and a third of an inch of ice accumulation was reported in the county. The ice accumulated mainly on trees and grassy surfaces. Some trees and limbs were toppled by the ice and there were also a few reports of power outages. A few accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located across northern Ohio on April 10th with temperatures ranging from 50 degrees near Lake Erie to near 80 degrees in Central Ohio. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening as a mid-level jet streak interacted with moderately unstable conditions in place south of the front. Reports of wind damage extended from Wyandot County in Northwest Ohio to Trumbull County in Northeast Ohio with numerous reports of downed trees limbs and power lines.  One of the more noteworthy reports came from Upper Sandusky in Wyandot County where four semi-trucks were blown over.  Several buildings also sustained minor damage to roofs and windows in Crawford, Richland, Summit, Portage, and Stark Counties.  The peak wind measured during the storms was 64 mph measured at an AWOS sensor at the Portage County Airport in Ravenna.  One building collapsed with three others sustaining damage at the Portage County Aiport grounds.  In Stark County, a dugout at Marlington High School was also destroyed by the wind.  Farther to the south, a swath of quarter to half dollar size hail and heavy rain extended from near Marion to Canton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported that four semi trucks were blown over along with numerous power lines and trees down across Upper Sandusky.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed during the afternoon heating south of a warm front. Some of these storms became severe during the afternoon and lasted into the evening. The main threats from these storms were large hail and isolated damaging winds. A few of these storms occurred over the same locations repeatedly, causing flash flooding to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roofing materials were removed from several buildings, a gutter was blown down, power lines were downed, and fencing was partially blown over in northeastern Columbus due to damaging thunderstorm winds. These winds also blew a large tree down onto a house on Schleppi Road.

Rain developed along this front.  Temperatures at the onset of the precipitation remained below freezing.  A four to six hour period of freezing rain occurred in the Youngstown-Warren area before temperatures warmed.  Further north the freezing precipitation lasted for a shorter duration.  Up to a third of an inch of ice accumulation was reported in Mahoning and Trumbull Counties with around a quarter inch in Portage and Ashtabula Counties.  The ice accumulated mainly on trees and grassy surfaces.  Strong southeast winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts as high as 35 mph reported.  Some trees and limbs were toppled by the ice and there were also a few reports of power outages.  A few accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A four to six hour period of freezing rain occurred across inland Ashtabula County.  Up to a quarter inch of ice accumulation was reported across the southern third to half of the county. The ice accumulated mainly on trees and grassy surfaces. Some trees and limbs were toppled by the ice and there were also a few reports of power outages. A few accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north, through southern Ohio, during the morning of the 12th.  Heating and instability increased during the afternoon with dew points reaching the lower 70s during the afternoon.  Disturbances in the mid and upper levels rippled southeast initially.  Addditional disturbances then moved east overnight Wednesday night.||Showers and thunderstorms formed over northern Ohio early on the evening of the 12th, and streaked southeast.   The southern edge of this convection finally sank down into portions of southeast Ohio late at night.   A few storms were severe, causing wind damage. ||Meanwhile, a separate large cluster of showers and thunderstorms moved out of northern Indiana into southeast Ohio during the predawn hours.  The southern edge of that convection became oriented west to east along the fast flow aloft.  Training of showers did cause 1.5 to 2 inches of rain across portions of Jackson, Vinton, Athens, Meigs and northern Gallia Counties.   Due to the relatively dry ground, no serious flash flooding occurred.  Some street flooding was observed in towns such as Athens and Gallipolis.  ||The final round of convection, associated with the cold front, streaked through southeast Ohio during the late morning hours on the 13th.  This caused another brief downpour with pea size hail and wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph.  No significant damage occurred.  Jackson Ohio reported about .9 inches in just over an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches fell onto power lines near Church Street.

One building collapsed with three others sustaining damage at the Portage County Aiport grounds.  In Stark County, a dugout at Marlington High School was also destroyed by the wind.  Farther to the south, a swath of quarter to half dollar size hail and heavy rain extended from near Marion to Canton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported quarter size hail southwest of Millersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed during the afternoon heating south of a warm front. Some of these storms became severe during the afternoon and lasted into the evening. The main threats from these storms were large hail and isolated damaging winds. A few of these storms occurred over the same locations repeatedly, causing flash flooding to occur.

An area of low pressure moved north across western Ohio on March 18th.  A warm front stretching east from the low also lifted north across the region.  Rain developed along this front.  Temperatures at the onset of the precipitation remained below freezing.  A four to six hour period of freezing rain occurred in the Youngstown-Warren area before temperatures warmed.  Further north the freezing precipitation lasted for a shorter duration.  Up to a third of an inch of ice accumulation was reported in Mahoning and Trumbull Counties with around a quarter inch in Portage and Ashtabula Counties.  The ice accumulated mainly on trees and grassy surfaces.  Strong southeast winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts as high as 35 mph reported.  Some trees and limbs were toppled by the ice and there were also a few reports of power outages.  A few accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A four to six hour period of freezing rain occurred in Mahoning County.  Up to a third of an inch of ice accumulation was reported in the county. The ice accumulated mainly on trees and grassy surfaces. Some trees and limbs were toppled by the ice and there were also a few reports of power outages. A few accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Sunday and Monday March 24th and 25th, snow and sleet along with some rain spread through the region, with significant snow found over metropolitan Dayton and to the northwest. Surface low pressure moved from the Tennessee Valley to Ohio during the overnight. This low got added influence with an upper low tracking east through Kentucky during the late night and early part of Monday. The system occluded over the state and large snowfall amounts were recorded north and west of Dayton. Drier air in the occlusion was pulled into central Ohio, limiting snowfall totals significantly in this area. Much of the early part of the event started as rain in the southern portion of the CWA and snow totals were notably lower in southern Ohio, southeast Indiana, and northern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county garage 3 miles west of Celina measured 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located across northern Ohio on April 10th with temperatures ranging from 50 degrees near Lake Erie to near 80 degrees in Central Ohio. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening as a mid-level jet streak interacted with moderately unstable conditions in place south of the front. Reports of wind damage extended from Wyandot County in Northwest Ohio to Trumbull County in Northeast Ohio with numerous reports of downed trees limbs and power lines.  One of the more noteworthy reports came from Upper Sandusky in Wyandot County where four semi-trucks were blown over.  Several buildings also sustained minor damage to roofs and windows in Crawford, Richland, Summit, Portage, and Stark Counties.  The peak wind measured during the storms was 64 mph measured at an AWOS sensor at the Portage County Airport in Ravenna.  One building collapsed with three others sustaining damage at the Portage County Aiport grounds.  In Stark County, a dugout at Marlington High School was also destroyed by the wind.  Farther to the south, a swath of quarter to half dollar size hail and heavy rain extended from near Marion to Canton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported several tree limbs down from strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the muggy summer environment, a complex of showers and thunderstorms formed during the evening hours in central Ohio.  The individual cells moved southeast.  Repetitive showers hit a small portion of eastern Perry County and western Morgan County.||Maximum rain estimates were on the order of 3 to 3.5 inches in around 4 hours from 2200E on the 8th through about 0200E on the 9th.   Measured amounts from 2 gauges around New Lexington had 2.2 inches of rain.||A new round of convection formed during the mid and late afternoon and moved into southeastern Ohio after 1600E.  This caused a second round of high water.  The combined effects brought Duck Creek and the Little Muskingum River in Washington County over its banks.   The second round also helped cause the lower reaches of Sunday Creek to cause some minor flooding in Athens County.   ||One unofficial measurement from a spotter located 4 miles northwest of Corning had a 30 hour total of 6.25 inches of rain from the afternoon of the 8th into the evening on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With repetitive rounds of showers and thunderstorms, Duck Creek rose late on the 9th and reached bankfull by the 10th.  The stream gauge at Whipple crested at 16.39 feet during the evening of the 10th.  Yet, the creek remained in flood through most of the 11th.||Numerous roads were blocked, including Route 821 south of Whipple and Route 145 near Lower Salem.  This caused residents to seek alternate routes. One county road was damaged by the high water.  One person was evacuated as a precaution, as water surrounded her house.  Water from a feeder creek was 8 to 10 inches deep in a fire station.||Other creeks, like Cat Creek near Lowell, also flooded some roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong frontal system remained nearly stationary across the Upper Ohio Valley during the evening hours of June 12th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across northern Ohio early in the evening. The storms became more numerous as the evening progressed. Many of the storms became severe. Dozens of reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Scattered power outages resulted from these storms.||A second round of strong thunderstorms occurred after midnight.  A long lived bowing line of storms or Derecho developed to the west of the area.  This line crossed the I-75 corridor just after midnight and then raced east across the remainder of the northern Ohio during the early morning hours.  Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph occurred across most of the area resulting in considerable damage and scattered power outages.  A weak tornado also occurred in Wood County as the line moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed six trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front that set up just north of I-80 became the focus for convection on the afternoon of April 10th as a shortwave riding along the front approached. Several lines of showers and thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail as they sank southeastward across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported that a large tree was blown down onto a residence in Dover.

Dozens of reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported. Scattered power outages resulted from these storms.||A second round of strong thunderstorms occurred after midnight.  A long lived bowing line of storms or Derecho developed to the west of the area.  This line crossed the I-75 corridor just after midnight and then raced east across the remainder of the northern Ohio during the early morning hours.  Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph occurred across most of the area resulting in considerable damage and scattered power outages.  A weak tornado also occurred in Wood County as the line moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid airmass was in place on May 31st.  Showers and thunderstorms developed across northern Ohio.  Some of the stronger thunderstorms became severe.  Damage was reported in several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.  Several large tree limbs were downed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down about a mile west of Custar and then traveled east.  The tornado lifted as it entered the west end of Custar.   Some trees were toppled along the damage path and two homes and a garage in Custar sustained roof damage.  Damaging straight line winds accompanied the tornado and downed trees elsewhere in Custar.  The tornado had a damage path nearly a mile in length and up to 50 yards in width.  This tornado was rated an EF0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms with heavy rain trained over Cuyahoga and Lorain Counties during the evening hours of the 25th. Instantaneous rainfall rates around 7 inches/hour were estimated by dual pol radar, with a storm total rainfall estimated around 4 inches between 9 and 11 pm. The heaviest rain fell over Columbia Station and Olmsted Township in eastern Lorain and southwest Cuyahoga Counties respectively. The highest measured rainfall was southeast of Elyria of 3.81.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rainfall caused about a 2 mile stretch of State Route 82 to flood with as much as  a foot of flowing water in Columbia station. At Snell Road and State Route 82 three motorists were pulled from a flooded intersection. A home on Parkwood Drive just south of Sprague Road and the Cuyahoga County line a home was inundated with a few feet of water on the first floor as the Baker Creek went from 6 feet wide to an estimated 500 feet wide onto the owners property. Several homes in Columbia Station, Olmsted Township, and Berea had basement flooding. In Berea, over 2 dozen homes were inundated when the sewer backed up from the overwhelmed storm drains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several days of showers and thunderstorms the ground cover in Ashland County was near saturation. A particularly brief but intense thunderstorm moved into the county between 8 and 9 PM with rainfall rates over 4 inches per hour. The torrential nature of the rain overwhelmed area creeks and stream and flooded several low lying areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State route 89 was flooded in half a dozen spots and was closed. Several roads in Ashland were closed. Some local roads were washed out with the pavement removed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved into the Ohio Valley from the plains over the weekend of the 22nd, bringing heavy rain and severe storms to central and northern Ohio. An unusually strong jet transported nearly historic amounts of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico into the region which pushed surface dew points to 60 degrees by Saturday evening. The warm air and strong winds aloft supported the growth of strong damaging storms and heavy rain. Most of the region measured between two to four inches of precipitation. The recently melted snow pack combined with runoff from the moderate to heavy rain that weekend resulted in widespread flooding with all rivers experiencing minor to major flooding.  A warm front lifted north across the area during the evening hours.  Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late evening hours of the 21st.  Severe weather continued into the early morning hours of the 22nd.  A lot of wind damage was produced by the severe thunderstorms.  The damage was especially bad across northern Richland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain around 2 inches in 6 hours caused the Whetstone Creek to flash flood during the early morning hours of the 22nd. The Whetstone Creek runs from the Mt. Gilead Lakes and Mt. Gilead Upper Lake Dam through southern Mt. Gilead. A dog shelter just west of Main Street was flooded with 18 of water from the flood. The dogs had been removed before the flood waters crested. The Morrow County Fairgrounds inundated as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass was in place across Lake Erie and Northeast Ohio from January 21 to Januray 24 resulting in periods of heavy lake effect snow in the snowbelt.  Minor synoptic snow began on the night of January 20th with snow increasing during the early morning hours of Januray 21 as west to east lake effect snow bands developed along the lakeshore.  Snow intensified on the 21st with a dominant west to east band bringing extremely heavy snow across portions of the east Cleveland suburbs during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Snowfall rates approached 3 inches per hour for a period of time with near zero visibilities.  Snow bands on January 22nd became more transitory, then re-organized again on the evening the of the 22nd. Drier air finally started to work into the area from the west on Januray 23rd with the heavier snow shifting northeast towards Ashtabula County.  Snow finally ended in extreme northeast Ohio on the morning of January 24th.  ||Lake, Geauga, and Ashtabula Counties were especially hard hit by this storm.  Some of the noteworthy snowfall totals included 26.3 inches at Thompson in northern Geauga County; 21.7 inches at Montville in Geauga County; 21.5 inches in South Madison in Lake County and 15.3 inches in Saybrook in Ashtabula County.||In addition to the snowfall, westerly winds of 15 to 25 mph with gusts to 35 mph persisted on January 21st and 22nd.  The combination of arctic air and gusty winds caused dangerous wind chill conditions with values as low as -15 degrees.  The gusty winds also caused some blowing and drifting of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed during the early morning hours of January 21st and continued into the early morning hours of the 24th. The snow was heavy at times. A peak total of 26.3 Inches was measured at Thompson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two fast moving systems combined over the region as a southern low pressure system was absorbed by a stronger low pressure system over the Great Lakes region. The lift between the two systems produced an average of 2 to 3 inches of snow between Dayton, Wapakoneta, and Columbus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two miles west of Greenville, a member of the public measured 2.5 inches of snow. The cooperative observer in Versailles measured 2 inches, while the county garage east of Greenville measured an 1.5 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county garage northeast of Delaware measured 3 inches of snow. A spotter in town measured 2.8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system combined with an unseasonably warm airmass to produce organized storms across the region. These storms were tornadic across Illinois and western Indiana, and began to transition to non-tornadic storms as they entered eastern Indiana. The main threat from these storms when they arrived across southwestern Ohio was damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off of a barn near the Union and Marion County line due to thunderstorm winds.

An unusually strong jet transported nearly historic amounts of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico into the region which pushed surface dew points to 60 degrees by Saturday evening. The warm air and strong winds aloft supported the growth of strong damaging storms and heavy rain. Most of the region measured between two to four inches of precipitation. The recently melted snow pack combined with runoff from the moderate to heavy rain that weekend resulted in widespread flooding with all rivers experiencing minor to major flooding.  A warm front lifted north across the area during the evening hours.  Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late evening hours of the 21st.  Severe weather continued into the early morning hours of the 22nd.  A lot of wind damage was produced by the severe thunderstorms.  The damage was especially bad across northern Richland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Scioto River at La Rue in Marion County Ohio experienced it's worst flood in 50 years (03/05/63 15.95 feet) when it crested at a stage of 14.18 feet on December 22nd. This major flood surpassed the more recent floods February 28, 2011 by only 0.09 feet. The Scioto River will flood the outskirts of town before reaching the city limits. When the river comes out of banks, it quickly fills up the low lying town. The Kiwanis Village retirement community, an apartment complex for seniors inundated as much as 2 feet in some units, and reached the roofs of cars left in the parking lot.  100 people had to be evacuated. Access in and out of town were cut off by flood waters. The floods were the result of heavy rain tha fell on December 21st over saturated ground. Any snow had long since melted and the ground was not frozen. The basin average rainfall was around 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass was in place across Lake Erie and Northeast Ohio from January 21 to Januray 24 resulting in periods of heavy lake effect snow in the snowbelt.  Minor synoptic snow began on the night of January 20th with snow increasing during the early morning hours of Januray 21 as west to east lake effect snow bands developed along the lakeshore.  Snow intensified on the 21st with a dominant west to east band bringing extremely heavy snow across portions of the east Cleveland suburbs during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Snowfall rates approached 3 inches per hour for a period of time with near zero visibilities.  Snow bands on January 22nd became more transitory, then re-organized again on the evening the of the 22nd. Drier air finally started to work into the area from the west on Januray 23rd with the heavier snow shifting northeast towards Ashtabula County.  Snow finally ended in extreme northeast Ohio on the morning of January 24th.  ||Lake, Geauga, and Ashtabula Counties were especially hard hit by this storm.  Some of the noteworthy snowfall totals included 26.3 inches at Thompson in northern Geauga County; 21.7 inches at Montville in Geauga County; 21.5 inches in South Madison in Lake County and 15.3 inches in Saybrook in Ashtabula County.||In addition to the snowfall, westerly winds of 15 to 25 mph with gusts to 35 mph persisted on January 21st and 22nd.  The combination of arctic air and gusty winds caused dangerous wind chill conditions with values as low as -15 degrees.  The gusty winds also caused some blowing and drifting of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed during the early morning hours of January 21st and continued into the early morning hours of the 24th.  The snow was heavy at times.  A peak total of 15.3 inches was measured in Saybrook Township.  Much of that total fell on the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system combined with an unseasonably warm airmass to produce organized storms across the region. These storms were tornadic across Illinois and western Indiana, and began to transition to non-tornadic storms as they entered eastern Indiana. The main threat from these storms when they arrived across southwestern Ohio was damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roofing materials were blown off of two buildings due to thunderstorm winds.

A storm system moved into the Ohio Valley from the plains over the weekend of the 22nd, bringing heavy rain and severe storms to central and northern Ohio. An unusually strong jet transported nearly historic amounts of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico into the region which pushed surface dew points to 60 degrees by Saturday evening. The warm air and strong winds aloft supported the growth of strong damaging storms and heavy rain. Most of the region measured between two to four inches of precipitation. The recently melted snow pack combined with runoff from the moderate to heavy rain that weekend resulted in widespread flooding with all rivers experiencing minor to major flooding.  A warm front lifted north across the area during the evening hours.  Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late evening hours of the 21st.  Severe weather continued into the early morning hours of the 22nd.  A lot of wind damage was produced by the severe thunderstorms.  The damage was especially bad across northern Richland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed many trees.   Several homes lost shingles and siding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across the northern half of Richland County and caused widespread damage.  The damage was especially bad in the Shelby area where dozens of trees were downed causing widespread power outages.  At least four building sustained significant damage from either the strong winds or fallen trees.  Dozens of other homes lost roofing or siding.  Several barns to the east of Shelby were leveled.  A lot of damage was also reported north of the Mansfield Airport in the Ganges Five Points area where at least four farm buildings were destroyed.  A large sign was flattened at the intersection of State Route 13 and 96.   Dozens of trees were downed elsewhere across northern Richland County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two fast moving systems combined over the region as a southern low pressure system was absorbed by a stronger low pressure system over the Great Lakes region. The lift between the two systems produced an average of 2 to 3 inches of snow between Dayton, Wapakoneta, and Columbus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Columbus airport measured 3.3 inches of snow. A member of the public measured 3.2 inches in Dublin, a local news station measured 3 inches east of Upper Arlington as did a spotter north of Columbus, and another public report of 2.7 inches came in from the south side of Columbus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two fast moving systems combined over the region as a southern low pressure system was absorbed by a stronger low pressure system over the Great Lakes region. The lift between the two systems produced an average of 2 to 3 inches of snow between Dayton, Wapakoneta, and Columbus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local news station measured 3 inches of snow in Cedarville. A spotter measured 2.5 inches in Beavercreek, while the highway department measured 1.5 inches east of Xenia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain showers began during the morning hours on the 5th, ahead of an approaching cold front.  In the wake of the frontal passage during the afternoon, the showers transformed into periods of rain.  The cold rain continued during the morning and into the afternoon on the 6th. The 24 to 30 hour rain event amounted to over 2 inches in some spots of southeast Ohio.  Streams were swollen, but significant flooding did not occur.||The rain changed to sleet, then to a period of snow for the evening hours, as a low pressure wave rippled along the front.  The snow quickly diminished by late evening.||The most snow accumulation was in Perry County. Snow accumulations of 3 to 5 inches were observed there in a short period of time.  For example, the cooperative observer in New Lexington and a spotter near Somerset both measured 4 inches of snow on the ground.||Further to the south and east, the Ohio counties only had 2 inches or less of snow accumulation.

Low pressure originating along the Gulf Coast on November 26th tracked northeast up the east side of the Appalachians during the early hours of November 27th.  Heavy snow overspread Northeast Ohio as moisture associated with this system interacted with cold air over the lower Great Lakes.  Snow began late in the morning of the 26th and continued into the afternoon hours of the 27th.  The snow was heavy at times, especially during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 26th when snowfall rates were in excess of an inch per hour.   Accumulations of greater than six inches were reported over most of Stark, Portage, Mahoning and Trumbull Counties.  The greatest accumulations were in southern and eastern Mahoning County where spotters reported between 9 and 12 inches of snow in the Poland and Boardman areas.  Some freezing drizzle briefly mixed with the snow during the predawn hours of the 27th.  A slight glaze of ice was reported in a few areas.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph at times causing blowing and drifting.  Travel was difficult during this event and many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began late in the morning of the 26th and continued into the morning hours of the 27th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved into the Ohio Valley from the plains over the weekend of the 22nd, bringing heavy rain and severe storms to central and northern Ohio. An unusually strong jet transported nearly historic amounts of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico into the region which pushed surface dew points to 60 degrees by Saturday evening. The warm air and strong winds aloft supported the growth of strong damaging storms and heavy rain. Most of the region measured between two to four inches of precipitation. The recently melted snow pack combined with runoff from the moderate to heavy rain that weekend resulted in widespread flooding with all rivers experiencing minor to major flooding.  A warm front lifted north across the area during the evening hours.  Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late evening hours of the 21st.  Severe weather continued into the early morning hours of the 22nd.  A lot of wind damage was produced by the severe thunderstorms.  The damage was especially bad across northern Richland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds tore the roof off of a building in Sullivan.  Two people inside the building were briefly trapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A highly unstable airmass produced deep convective snow showers that produced snow squalls during the late morning into the afternoon. These isolated squalls caused whiteout conditions on area roadways. Three major pileups and one minor pileup resulted in over 175 vehicle crashes on the interstate system, causing numerous injuries and one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls in the region caused whiteout conditions, resulting in a 29 car pileup in the southbound lanes of I-270 on the northeast side of Columbus. Four people were hospitalized as a result. Later in the afternoon, I-71 at the intersection of I-670 saw an accident involving two semi-trailers and other vehicles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms wind gusts in excess of 60 mph caused considerable damage in Norwalk.  Dozens of trees were downed and at least one home sustained structural damage.  Over half of the city lost power from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of January 30th, a convective line of showers formed along a pre-frontal surface trough and intensified as it moved east at 50 MPH. While only isolated thunderstorms occurred, the line produced convective wind damage across portions of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia. The highest measured wind gusts were 67 MPH at Arnold Palmer Regional Airport in Latrobe, and 62 MPH at the Wheeling, WV airport. Heavy rainfall with this system caused some flooding of roadways over eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia the evening of the 30th into the morning of the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported State Route 147 had flooding due to standing water near Barnesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure originating along the Gulf Coast on November 26th tracked northeast up the east side of the Appalachians during the early hours of November 27th.  Heavy snow overspread Northeast Ohio as moisture associated with this system interacted with cold air over the lower Great Lakes.  Snow began late in the morning of the 26th and continued into the afternoon hours of the 27th.  The snow was heavy at times, especially during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 26th when snowfall rates were in excess of an inch per hour.   Accumulations of greater than six inches were reported over most of Stark, Portage, Mahoning and Trumbull Counties.  The greatest accumulations were in southern and eastern Mahoning County where spotters reported between 9 and 12 inches of snow in the Poland and Boardman areas.  Some freezing drizzle briefly mixed with the snow during the predawn hours of the 27th.  A slight glaze of ice was reported in a few areas.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph at times causing blowing and drifting.  Travel was difficult during this event and many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began late in the morning of the 26th and continued into the morning hours of the 27th. The snow was heavy at times, especially during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 26th when snowfall rates were in excess of an inch per hour. Accumulations of greater than six inches were reported over most of Mahoning County. The greatest accumulations were in southern and eastern Mahoning County where spotters reported between 9 and 12 inches of snow in the Poland and Boardman areas. Some freezing drizzle briefly mixed with the snow during the predawn hours of the 27th. A slight glaze of ice was reported in a few areas. Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph at times causing blowing and drifting. Travel was difficult during this event and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system combined with an unseasonably warm airmass to produce organized storms across the region. These storms were tornadic across Illinois and western Indiana, and began to transition to non-tornadic storms as they entered eastern Indiana. The main threat from these storms when they arrived across southwestern Ohio was damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a car with people inside. No injuries occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass was in place across Lake Erie and Northeast Ohio from January 21 to Januray 24 resulting in periods of heavy lake effect snow in the snowbelt.  Minor synoptic snow began on the night of January 20th with snow increasing during the early morning hours of Januray 21 as west to east lake effect snow bands developed along the lakeshore.  Snow intensified on the 21st with a dominant west to east band bringing extremely heavy snow across portions of the east Cleveland suburbs during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Snowfall rates approached 3 inches per hour for a period of time with near zero visibilities.  Snow bands on January 22nd became more transitory, then re-organized again on the evening the of the 22nd. Drier air finally started to work into the area from the west on Januray 23rd with the heavier snow shifting northeast towards Ashtabula County.  Snow finally ended in extreme northeast Ohio on the morning of January 24th.  ||Lake, Geauga, and Ashtabula Counties were especially hard hit by this storm.  Some of the noteworthy snowfall totals included 26.3 inches at Thompson in northern Geauga County; 21.7 inches at Montville in Geauga County; 21.5 inches in South Madison in Lake County and 15.3 inches in Saybrook in Ashtabula County.||In addition to the snowfall, westerly winds of 15 to 25 mph with gusts to 35 mph persisted on January 21st and 22nd.  The combination of arctic air and gusty winds caused dangerous wind chill conditions with values as low as -15 degrees.  The gusty winds also caused some blowing and drifting of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed during the early morning hours of January 21st and continued into the early morning hours of the 24th. The snow was heavy at times. A peak total of 21.5 inches was measured at South Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A highly unstable airmass produced deep convective snow showers that produced snow squalls during the late morning into the afternoon. These isolated squalls caused whiteout conditions on area roadways. Three major pileups and one minor pileup resulted in over 175 vehicle crashes on the interstate system, causing numerous injuries and one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls in the region caused whiteout conditions, resulting in a 90 car pileup on I-275 on the northwest side of Cincinnati. The accident has been called the largest vehicle crash in Ohio history, involving 18 semi-trailers and 72 cars, trucks, and SUVs. A 12 year old girl was killed and 27 others were injured. The fatality occurred when a vehicle hit a median barrier made of wire. The girl was farther up the highway and outside of her vehicle that was involved in the pileup. When the car hit the wire barrier, the wire snapped further up the road and the girl was killed when it recoiled into her.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across northern Ohio during the evening hours of November 17th.  A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed in advance of this front causing significant damage in some areas.  At least three tornadoes touched down in Wood County.  The strongest of the three, an EF2, crossed into Lucas County.  A fourth tornado occurred in Ottawa County.  All of the tornadoes downed trees and damaged homes and buildings.  Strong non thunderstorm winds also occurred behind the cold front through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed nine large trees along Woodlawn Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across northern Ohio during the evening hours of November 17th.  A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed in advance of this front causing significant damage in some areas.  At least three tornadoes touched down in Wood County.  The strongest of the three, an EF2, crossed into Lucas County.  A fourth tornado occurred in Ottawa County.  All of the tornadoes downed trees and damaged homes and buildings.  Strong non thunderstorm winds also occurred behind the cold front through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree on State Route 113.  Several other trees were brought down by thunderstorm winds on State Route 57.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved into the Ohio Valley from the plains over the weekend of the 22nd, bringing heavy rain and severe storms to central and northern Ohio. An unusually strong jet transported nearly historic amounts of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico into the region which pushed surface dew points to 60 degrees by Saturday evening. The warm air and strong winds aloft supported the growth of strong damaging storms and heavy rain. Most of the region measured between two to four inches of precipitation. The recently melted snow pack combined with runoff from the moderate to heavy rain that weekend resulted in widespread flooding with all rivers experiencing minor to major flooding.  A warm front lifted north across the area during the evening hours.  Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late evening hours of the 21st.  Severe weather continued into the early morning hours of the 22nd.  A lot of wind damage was produced by the severe thunderstorms.  The damage was especially bad across northern Richland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warm rain of around an inch to an inch and a half melted all the snowpack during the 24 hours preceding the flash floods. The earlier rain saturated the ground and filled the storm drainage systems in the city of Tiffin. As a round of moderate rainfall moved in on the evening of the 22nd, it produced overflow and flooding in the city. The viaduct on North Washington Street was flooded and closed, and Main Street near Heidelberg University's senior apartments was flooded. Areas throughout Seneca County experiencing high water were CR 594; SR 587, north of TR 96; SR 231, south of CR 6; SR 4, north and south of Attica; North SR 53 at CR 32; SR 101 at CR 179; SR 19 at CR 32; and Euclid Avenue. Also, US 224, east of SR 100, was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved into the Ohio Valley from the plains over the weekend of the 22nd, bringing heavy rain and severe storms to central and northern Ohio. An unusually strong jet transported nearly historic amounts of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico into the region which pushed surface dew points to 60 degrees by Saturday evening. The warm air and strong winds aloft supported the growth of strong damaging storms and heavy rain. Most of the region measured between two to four inches of precipitation. The recently melted snow pack combined with runoff from the moderate to heavy rain that weekend resulted in widespread flooding with all rivers experiencing minor to major flooding.  A warm front lifted north across the area during the evening hours.  Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late evening hours of the 21st.  Severe weather continued into the early morning hours of the 22nd.  A lot of wind damage was produced by the severe thunderstorms.  The damage was especially bad across northern Richland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sandusky River at Tiffin came out of its banks after a heavy rain event on the 21st and 22nd. The river 11.11 feet, 0.61 feet above moderate flood stage. Flooding impacted Route 53 north of U.S. 224. Floodwaters also inundated Township Road 131. In the city of Tiffin, Water Street, Marlyn Street, Front Street, and Davis Street were inundated. There was one fatality on Maryln Street due to the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blanchard River at Findlay Ohio crested at 15.58 feet on Sunday the 22nd of December according to a USGS post storm survey. This crest is the 8th highest on record and two feet above major flood stage. The level, though significant, did not reach the level needed for massive evacuations which is 16 feet.Significant parts of Findlay were underwater and numerous state highways were closed. The Eagle Creek, a tributary to joins with the Blanchard River in Findlay, also reached major flood stage at 11.4 feet. The highways that were closed included U.S. 68 on the south side. Four homes sustained major damage, 2 minor based on FEMA's damage assessment criteria. Other homes were flooded in their basements. In all, 45 homes experienced flood damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system moved across the region, bringing strong winds aloft across the Ohio Valley. Showers that developed along and ahead of a cold front associated with this storm system were capable of producing damaging winds and isolated weak tornadoes. This line of showers producing the damage across southwestern Ohio continued past midnight on October 31st and into the early morning hours of November 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down in a corn field on the west side of Lena-Palestine Road where it produced a clear path of heavily damaged corn stalks. The tornado then crossed Lena-Palestine Road where it destroyed a barn and strewn debris from the barn to the north and upwind of the actual tornado path. A large portion of the barn roof was lifted and thrown upwind into the top of a nearby tree. A tree was also split near this barn. The tornado then continued on the ground and produced heavy damage to a second barn north of the first barn before the tornado lifted. Based on the damage surveyed, the maximum wind speed of this tornado was estimated at 75 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon ahead of a strong low pressure system and a cold front. The primary threat from these storms was damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were downed near Manchester due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and isolated thunderstorms developed along a cold front across Ohio the evening of October 31st. This line moved east during the overnight hours of November 1st and was able to mix strong winds aloft down to the surface. Severe thunderstorm wind damage was reported across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia. At one time nearly 50 thousand homes were without power across the region due to downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down near Cambridge along old Route 21.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down and moved through a manufactured home community just north of Interstate 270 in southern Columbus. The tornado was on the ground for about a quarter of a mile before it lifted. During that time, 3 mobile homes were destroyed, 4 sustained major damage, and 23 sustained minor damage. The damage that occurred to some of the homes included partial roofs being torn off the buildings, and the units being shifted off of their foundations. Based on the damage surveyed, the maximum wind speed of this tornado was estimated to be 85 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure lifted north through the Chicago and the Lake Michigan vicinity during the late afternoon on Sunday the 17th.  A strong low level wind field existed in the Ohio Valley.  Extension clouds and showers fell during the day on Sunday well ahead of the associated cold front.  Surface surface dew points raised into the upper 50 degrees by nightfall on Sunday evening.  ||As the parent low pressure center lifted north toward Ontario, the associated front swept through southeast Ohio during the evening hours.  Narrow bands of showers, with just a few embedded thunderstorms, preceded the front.  The showers were moving at 50 to 60 mph.  Widespread wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph occurred.  A few locations had severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along Route 50.  Large branches fell in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system moved across the region, bringing strong winds aloft across the Ohio Valley. Showers that developed along and ahead of a cold front associated with this storm system were capable of producing damaging winds and isolated weak tornadoes. This line of showers producing the damage across southwestern Ohio continued past midnight on October 31st and into the early morning hours of November 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down just northeast of Interstate 75 at Highway 40, exit number 63 in Vandalia. Initial damage consisted of the collapse of a cinderblock drive through store, and significant destruction of the roof and some of the cinderblocks on an uninhabited commercial building. This structure also had large plate glass windows blown in. Additional significant roof damage occurred to a neighboring business, as well as the snapping of some power poles. The tornado continued on to a restaurant, in which the windows were blown in and significant fa��ade and roof damage occurred. Eight people inside the restaurant sustained minor injuries from glass. In the restaurant parking lot, there were numerous vehicles which had windows blown out, with some vehicles shifted or pushed from their parked location. There was a large pickup truck which was lifted and tossed, landing on top of another vehicle. The tornado continued into a residential subdivision, where at least 3 homes sustained damage which rendered them uninhabitable. There were additional homes with partial wall collapses and more than half the roof removed. Other homes had holes in the roof due to being impaled by debris. There was also significant damage due to large trees being snapped off and damaging the homes. Large portions of roofing and sheds were carried at least a city block. The tornado then lifted while in the residential subdivision. Based on the damage surveyed, the maximum wind speed of this tornado was estimated to be between 100 and 110 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and isolated thunderstorms developed along a cold front across Ohio the evening of October 31st. This line moved east during the overnight hours of November 1st and was able to mix strong winds aloft down to the surface. Severe thunderstorm wind damage was reported across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia. At one time nearly 50 thousand homes were without power across the region due to downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported trees down east of Coshocton.
